Ancient ruins of the Champa people, appreciated our tour guide for his knowledge as there are few English translations.  One of the most ancient buildings/ruins in Vietnam.  Much warmer here so bring your water.  Bomb craters from the Vietnam war in evidence and warnings not to stray from the guided paths as possibility of unexploded landmines in this area. Archaeologists in situ still discovering the secrets these buildings hold.

Interesting place to visit. Ancient ruins peppered with bomb craters. Whats not to lime. Reasonable entry fees too. Although not on the scale with Angkor Wat it is still a nice place to spend a few hours
Si vas a estar varios dias por Hoi an realizad esta visita. Nosotros fuimos en moto, pero aviso que es un trayecto de 1 hora aproximadamente. imprescindible ir con mapa. A nosotros nos gustó mucho.

Met de auto van Hoi An naar My Son. Daar rondleiding van twee uur gehad door onze gids. Veel is er jammer genoeg niet meer van over. Soort mini Ankor Wat. Wel mooi om te zien en ook mooie omgeving. Daarna naar een huis geweest waar ze rijstwafels stomen en daarna lunch gehad. Laatste deel met de boot terug. Al met al ruim zes uur op pad geweest. Goed georganiseerd.

Get there early , it was very hot and still in the jungle . Amazing place , our guide talked us through the Cham peoples place in Vietnam history  . Shame so few of the buildings , only rediscovered 90 years ago survived the US bombers during the war . Enjoyed the dancing show , and hope the building reconstruction continues .

Ce site niché au milieu de nul part est trés beau et agréable. Un guide nous a retracé l'histoire de ce sanctuaire qui malheureusement a subi beaucoup de destruction et n'a pas de fonds pour sa réhabilitation.Je pense qu'une visite avec un guide est nécessaire.
This UNESCO site was 70 % destroyed during the Vietnamese war by American bombing. What remains is imposing. Even now we’ll worth seeing

My Son is a rather little site (at least the visitabile bits!) with an awesome old history behind it. Is interesting to know about the alignment of the buildings, the American bombing of 1972 (shells can still be seen in one of the exposition areas!) and see the altars. Is nicely structured with buggies that take you around, beautiful nature and beautiful “red bricks” structure.Very interesting (it is considered the Angor Wat of Vietnam)

Peccato veramente per le bombe che lo hanno devastato, ma comunque possiamo visitare ancora alcune strutture, chiaramente senza copertura e reperti di questo luogo con forti influenze induiste. Ottimi spunti per foto carine.Il primo gruppo di strutture è molto affollato, secondo noi il momento migliore in cui andare è all’alba ( apre alle 6:30 ) o nel pomeriggio.

While these Champa ruins may not be as significant as Angkor Wat in Cambodia, they offer a great half-day trip from Hoi An. We decided Hue was simply too far from Hoi An for a day trip. Here we saw an excellent museum and then went to the ruins. Many are quite well-preserved and restored. A shuttle bus made for less walking. There were only a handful of other visitors there. Many of our photos had only me and my friend in them! We especially enjoyed the preserved monsters heads guarding the entrance to the temple.

A lot of the negative reviews here focus on the heat, crowds, and expensive tours. All these are within the persons control and can be easily avoided. I came to the site at dawn by rented scooter with a friend, and we were literally the first and only people to walk in at about 6:20 am. I had heard there were sunrise tours, but they were clearly late, as we had the whole place to ourselves until about 7am.Since it was still so quiet (even the parking attendant staff and restaurant staff had not yet arrived), I saw lots of beautiful bird varieties just while walking between the ruin blocks.The sounds of the birds was amazing, and lent a lot to the mystery of the ruins.The ruins themselves don't take too long to explore depending on what kind of guide book you are carrying (there were also no local guides available at the early hour). However, the museum is full of information and you can check it out afterwards.  I actually walked around the circuit twice, as it was cloudy at sunrise and there was better light a bit later at 9.  I caught a bit of the dance performance (not too exciting for me) before perusing the museum.  All in all took a leisurely half day, was back in My Son for lunch. Coffee inside near to the ruins is surprisingly not that overpriced (VND 20,000) so you can get your caffeine fix if you are here early. If you are someone who loves ancient ruins and imagining bygone civilizations at your own pace, rent your own scooter and get yourself here before the loud annoying crowds.  It felt vastly different by 10am when the parking lot was full of tour buses.Don't do a group tour unless you want to be rushed through! Plus you can drive back a different more rural route and see interesting sites on the way.
占婆王國用以祭祀君主和神靈的印度教聖都，是東南亞長期連續發現的唯一建築群體。下雨天欣賞古蹟也是另一個體驗，占婆人是砌磚藝術大師，密密的重疊，不用灰漿，至今尚未研究出所用的是什麼粘結劑，是個非常奇妙的地方。

Visited this sanctuary back in May 2019, weather was terribly hot. We bought tickets at the entrance, took a short walk in and gathered at the buggy pick-up area. The buggy will take you into the sanctuary, and from there you need to walk following the map. You won't get lost as the route is just a big circle. Make sure to wear hat and sunglasses, drink water to keep you hydrated. Not much shade or resting booth along the route. There was a small convenient hut at the end of the route to get yourself some ice cream and cold drinks.There were so many ancient ruins to explore and learn, sign posts with English explanation were provided.One minor issue we encountered during our trip while we were exploring, a local tour guide screamed at us telling us to leave the site. We thought we were not allow to enter one of the ruins, so we did what he told. Once everyone including other tourists cleared the scene, that tour guide told his group to take photos of the ruin without people in it. We complaint to the guard posting at that site immediately, but no action was taken. So disappointing!On the way out, we stopped by the mini museum, it was nice to see some of the artifacts displayed chronologically with information clearly explaining every piece. If you have elder travel mate, you can just leave them at the museum while you head into the sanctuary for a quick exploration. The museum is AC equipped.

ミーソン遺跡はチャンパ王国の宗教遺跡。3世紀頃は木造建築、7世紀以降は現在のれんが造りとなって残っている。ベトナム戦争によって一部が破壊されたが主要な部分は残っている。れんが造りの建築物は杭などを用いておらず、どのような仕組みで崩れずに建物として残っているかは未だに解明されていないとか。1999年に世界遺産に登録されたが、日本の支援が相当大きかったとガイドさんは言っていた。ダナンから少し距離はあるが幻想的な雰囲気を堪能できる。

ホテルでタクシーをチャーターしていきました。8時間で7500円ぐらい。まだ再建中で大きな期待を持って行くと残念かもしれないです。ダナンのリゾートでのんびりして、少しは遺跡的なものを見に行こうかなとちょっと思った時に行かれると良いと思います。皆さん遺跡によじのぼって写真を撮っておりました……。また破壊が進むと思いますが、監視している人も口うるさく言うことはなく。日本人がとても多く来ていたように思います。ツアーがお勧めです　来るのはダナンから結構距離がありました

Structures dating back to 300 AD have been reassambled here, remarkably standing the test of time. The site was used for religious ceremonies for kings and, while not as stunning as Roman ruins or ancient sculptures, is a fabulously intriguing historic site.The restoration that began in 1937 has made reasonable progress and continues today. The walk around the entire property is nice and the trip is worth the effort.

Historical place with a lot of old temples (ruins). A lot of history here. If possible visit the place without a guided tour. It will give you more time to wonder around the ruins and get a sense of the place.

Spend your time going thru the museum (the first thing to do once you enter).  It has many good photos and writeups.  After you continue on and cross a bridge, you come to the shuttle stop.  You take it for many minutes to the main site.  While there are several possible shuttle stops at the main site, the shuttle may skip all but the first -- so my wife had to walk farther than she wanted.  The ruins are just so-so.  The show had three dances which reminded us of Thailand.  One can get food near the show.

Un très beau site assez étendu qui vaut la peine d être visite avec un guide. Nous y étions à l ouverture et déjà il y avait du monde. Accès avec un petit train pour limiter la marche.Une boutique à l entrée (prise de Tickets) Une autre au stop du train (prix super intéressants)Prévoir  1 anti moustiques car on est en pleine jungle.Une salle de spectacle se trouve au debut du site : vous avons assisté à de belles danses.Wc à proximité

We chartered a car to visit My Son Sauntuary to fit the trip easier into our itinerary. We like historic places and found the walk and the relics very enjoyable. I know people usually spend 2 hours there , but we stayed for four hours without realizing the time passed. It is a must for a trip to Da Nang.

La visite mérite d'être guidée pour ne pas perdre une miette de l'histoire de ce site incroyable dont 70% à malheureusement été détruit par la guerre du Vietnam. Vous y apprendrez notamment que la composition du ciment-colle utilisé pour assembler les brique du temple n'a jamais pu être identifiée ni copiée à ce jour. Malgré l'humidité ambiante, ce ciment ne laisse pas passer l'air et les briques sont parfaitement celées. La composition de ces dernières n'est pas connue non plus. Elles sont légère et parfaitement imperméables.Quel dommage que la guerre ait fait autant de dégâts !!!

It is nice to see some of the history of Vietnam being preserved. The drop off point and is a fair way from the temples which I thought was sensible and they have shuttles to take you most of the way. The walk is easy and well paved.Temples have weathered but have also had restoration work. The entrance fee helps pay for the maintenance.You can get up close and sometimes inside these relatively small temples. Worth a visit.

My (pronounced as 'Mee') Son is an old ancient museums, temples. Story about Hindu, Vietnamese King. Suggest to read this background a little prior visiting. Raining during my visit. Please bring along umbrella, poncho if possible. 1 mini snack bar counter at the base. A buggy will bring you up to the start point (3-4 km away).

It’s interesting to see the history and learn about the various artifacts. The guide was very informative though our group was just too big. Would recommend a smaller tour. It’s a good piece of history (both good and bad) to learn.

Se siete indecisi se andare o no:andate.Noi avevamo due giorni a hoi an e oer riempire un po' il tempo abbiamo deciso di andare a My Son. Il posto in sé è un po' rovinato dai bombardamenti americani ma rimane molto affascinante anche grazie alla guida simpatica e preparata che ci ha dato vari spubti di  storia, cultura e architettura.La gita dura mezza giornata il che lascia tempo a un giro pomeridiano al mercato di Hoi An.

It’s a great place to visit while in Hoi An. A first hand example of the destruction of a heritage site by warfare, the ravages of which are still being attempted to be restored. A lot of Indian influence is evident here.
An Unesco heritage of induist temples unfortunately partially damaged by bombs in 1969. the location is particularly well preserved

La visite de ce site est incontournable. Y aller en basse saison : moins chaud et moins de touristes. Il faut compter deux heures pour une visite complète et la faire avec un guide local pour les infos.

I had heard that cruise ships in the the area send bus loads of tourists to My Son. The facility just cannot handle the volume of people. That said, the ruins provided fascinating insight into the Cham people and it was well worth a visit. I suggest you try and take in the free 15 minute cultural show. The highly talented performers were well worth watching.

I visited My Son Sanctuary which has been designated a UNESCO World Heritage protected site. This site was found by French archaeologists. It covers a very large area so essential to wear good walking shoes or sports shoes.The place also has a restaurant and place to relax.After a 30 minutes drive from Hoi An we reached the My Son Sanctuary in a valley surrounded by a Mountain range.This old Indian Temple site has various Structures in varied  stages of disrepair having sufferred from World War 2 bombings and Two Indo China wars USA due to the Vietcoms having taken refuge there and also affected by earthquake. Various huge Bomb craters are dotted around the Temple complex . Only some partly standing parts of Temples there giving a glimpse of the glorious  Architecture of the Indian Temples with its exqusitely carved Statues of lord Vishnu, Lord Ganesha, Lord Shiva and was the place of the Champa peoples in Vietnam built between 4 th to 13 th centuries. Some of the Temples are being rebuilt with Monetary support from Japan , Korea and India.

Interesting to see all the beautiful buildings and just to walk among them. Sad to learn that so much of the sanctuary took such a heavy beating in the war 1969. We traveled by motorcycle from Hoi An and the place was easy to find and everything else easy and accessible.

ホイアンのホテルから車をチャーターして行きました。たくさんツアーも出ているようでしたが、チャーターだと好きな時間に行くことができ、複数人であれば代金もそれ程高くありませんでしたのでお勧めです。ただ、遺跡に詳しい説明が見当たらなかったのでガイドさんがついていないと…という方はツアーの方がいいでしょう。遺跡は再建中ということでしたが、ベトナム戦争での破壊は大きく、雨風で風化も進むことを考えれば、見られる今のうちに行った方が良いと考えます。チケット売り場近くに大きな売店があり、お土産や水などが購入できます。その後、無料の電動カート（これが結構なスピードで、遊園地のアトラクションのようです）に乗り、遺跡の入り口に。そこから遺跡を周回するように作られている遊歩道を巡ります。 ゆっくり廻って2時間弱でしょうか。小さな売店とダンスショーを見られる場所もありますが、ショーは回数が少なく時間が決まっていますし、観客が20人にならないと行われないようです。雨季に行きましたが、晴れると大変暑かったです。水分持参は必須でしょう。

すべての遺跡を隅々まで見るにはだいぶ時間がかかります。よく歩くのでスニーカーもしくわ歩きやすい靴がオススメ。入場口からはカートに乗って移動します。山の中に遺跡へ続く道をわざわざ作った、という感じで風が抜けて気持ちよかったです。カート降りてから一箇所目の遺跡まで数分また歩きます。再建中で、完成したと思ったらレンガを盗まれてまた未完成…。そこがいいのかもしれないですね…。遺跡中に入って写真を撮れるところもあります。途中と最後に踊り子のショーがありました。最後の売店まではトイレもないので、全て入場口で済ませてから入るべきです。入場料150,000VNDと少し高めですが、再建に役立てば…

Mỹ Sơn is regarded as one of the foremost Hindu temple complexes in SEA. The Hindu temples were constructed between the 4th and 14th century by the kings of Champa. The abandoned and partially ruined temples were dedicated to the worship of the Shiva, aka Bhadreshvara. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1999Be aware: busloads of tourists can overcrowd this beautiful site.

Hallo ihr Lieben,Ich war mit meinen Freunden in My Son. Wir sind mit einem privaten Fahrer angereist ohne eine Tour. Der Eintritt kostet 150.000 VND. Gleich am Anfang haben sie auf der rechten Seite ein Museum mit einigen Hintergrundinformationen zu der Anlage und wie sie restauriert wurde. Sowie Erklärungen zu den verschieden Statuen und Skulpturen. Ich würde das Museum zuerst anschauen und danach die Tempelanlage besichtigen, da auf der Anlage selbst sehr wenig Infotafeln vorhanden sind. Ein Stück weiter nach dem Museum kommt der Shutteltransfer, da es 2 km bis zur Anlage sind. Auf ein Shuttel muss man nicht warten. Es fährt regelmäßig, so bald es meist halb voll mit Leuten ist. Auf der Anlage angekommen führt ein Weg, wie ein Rundweg von einem Tempel zum Nächsten. Die Anlage ist zeitweise gut besucht, aber sehr schön. Es wurde zwar restauriert, jedoch nur das Nötigste. Dies macht die Tempelanlage sehr authentisch. Die Anlage selbst liegt im "Dschungel " umrahmt von Bergen. Ein seh schöner Hintergrund für eine solche Anlage. Sie ist nicht sehr weitläufig und gut zu Fuß zu erkunden. An der Anlage und am Eingang selbst gab es Bistros mit Essen und Trinken, sowie die landesüblichen Toiletten.Das Essen und Trinken kann ich nicht beurteilen bzw etwas zu den Preisen sagen, da wir nichts gekauft haben. Ich kann einen Besuch nur Empfehlen. So viel ich weiß kann man My Son auch als Tour buchen oder einen Audioguite bei der Anlage erwerben.Viel Spaß beim besichtigen.

Während unserer Rundreise haben wir diese kleine Tempelanlage als Tour dazugebucht. Hammer, ein Erlebnis. Kaum Touristen dort, nicht so gross wie andere Tempelanlagen, aber super sehenswert mit geschichtlichem Einblick und kleinem Museum. Shuttle zu den Tempeln, sonst 2 km zu Fuss.

My Son WHS is a large archeological sit at about 45 minutes from Hoi An. It is a great half-day visit, but tour packages are rather dry and limited in the services they offer. Do not bother to pay the extra money for the boat ride back to Hoi An with lunch (it was our worst meal in Vietnam - they serve you cold steamed with rice with a few green beans and no drink; you can tell how they try to save money: everyone in our group was stunned).

ダナンからGrabで往復セットで行きました。往復5000円程度だったか。ゲートで入場券を買って、少し行った所から電気バスで遺跡エリアまで向かいます。幾つかのエリアに遺跡が点在してますが、まだまだ再建中のエリアもありますので、何年か後に行くとまた変化しているでしょう。歩く距離が長いので暑い時は気をつけましょう。売店もいくつかあるので水分補給は可能です。ダンスショーは一日4回程度の開催ですが、時間が合わず観られませんでした。

We are amazed how innovative the cham people were being able to build from those bricks. The cultural show was also fun. Challenging to get photos because of tourists but be there between 12pm-2pm to acoid touristy crowds. The food at the restaurant was also acceptable. Lots of choices, toilet is clean. Parking for 5K is also available.

It’s a miracle there are any of these ancient ruins left at all when you see the size of the bomb craters throughout the park. Many of the structures have been repaired after hundreds of years of worship. It’s an interesting area to walk through. Electric cars will take you from the car park to the site. There is a show held intermittently throughout the day of traditional music and dance. Paths can be slippery to take care.

Si es que te quedas mas de un dia en hoi an puede ser una buenas excursion. Nosotros fuimos en moto desde hoi an, con una buena moto son unos 45/60 min. Recomendamos ir pronto por la mañana que no hay nadie, cuando nos ibamos a las 11 entravan 20 autobuses y eso no da para mucho.
Wir waren mit dem Taxi auf eigene Faust hin und haben fast zwei Stunden dort verbracht. Sehr beeindruckend!

Visiting the ancient sanctuary was really worth it. We enjoyed the monumental beauty and its history. Everything was well explained in the exposition and on the location itself. The Vietnamese people also started to conserve the heritage better because they know its importance which is a good thing.

Il faut visiter le musée pour profiter pleinement du site. Ce mini Ankor, témoigne de la culture passée du Vietnam. Au beau milieu de la jungle, ces vestiges de temple de briques rouges, malheureusement pillés en grande partie, semblent défier le temps et les lois de l’équilibre.

This site has about a dozen or so temples in restoration, many with interesting details.  It was heavily bombed by th U.S in the war, the reason being it was a Viet Cong  military field HQ, which they don't mention.  There are numerous ponds and craters left from the bombing.  Our visit lasted about an hour.  Go early as there were hords of people waiting for the tram by the time we left.
佔婆教曾對東南亞有相當大的影響力，大部分遊客已經忘記。這兒仍有一點點留下之遺跡，回顧過來建築物。從峴港出發，車程大約1.5小時抵達。

over 1000 years old remains of the Champa village and temples. Mostly all ruins and restructured. Lots of history to see and feel. Enjoyed their cultural dance performance at the end of the tour. Took a buggy ride to mid way and had to walk quite a bit to come to this spot.

Sito molto rovinato anche a causa dei bombardamenti durante la guerra del Vietnam ma inserito in un bellissimo contesto: circondato da colline e immerso nella giungla. Per evitare il flusso di turisti cinesi evitate di andare a My Son di mattina; meglio il pomeriggio dopo le 15 o la mattina prima della 10. Seguendo il mio consiglio vi godrete con calma l'atmosfera di pace e silenzio del sito e riuscirete davvero a immaginare il perché i Champa hanno scelto questa zona come luogo di culto.

Went with husband and three kids- 11,9,7. Hired a private car, it was about an hour and a bit from Hoi An. We got there about 8am, was quite a warm morning so took water and fans with us.We all really enjoyed the walk and checking out the ancient religious sites of the Cham people. It took us nearly 2 hours to get around the sites.Definitely worked out better getting their early as less crowded, quite a few buses were arriving as we left about midday. Definitely recommend visiting My Son.

6世紀から13世紀まで栄えたチャンパ王国の遺産です。ベトナム戦争で大半が破壊されてしまったため一部しか残っていませんが、それでも見応えがあります。朽ちたレンガに生えた苔、土と草の匂い。別の世界にタイムスリップしたような雰囲気を味わうことができます。全ての遺跡群を見るには3時間程度必要です。無料でチャムダンスショーを観賞できます。※10月下旬でもかなり暑いのでしっかり対策して行ってください。小さな売店もありますが、3キロの道のりで1店舗しか見当たりませんでしたので、水などは持参した方がいいです。

Visited this world heritage site this morning with our guide from Hoi An. Took just over an hour to get there. Weather was awful. Torrential rain. Sanctuary is very well organised. Visited museum first which gave background to Cham civilianisation morning place in Vietnamese history . Then proceeded to walk round the site and visit the excavated and restored site . An amazing feat of construction which was built from 4th century onwards and survived many wars. However much damage done by bombs in 1975 Vietnam war. Saw some traditional music and dance before we left. Takes 2 hours approx.

This place is a wonderful place to see an ancient city made from a group of vietnamese tribe. Full of architecture and history. This place was heavily bombed during the war which you can see as you walk around the sanctuary which makes it a sad and enjoyable experience.

10月に行きましたが、非常に暑かったです。合計3kmくらい歩きましたが、思った以上に体力を消耗しました。日陰が少ないので、帽子必須です！ベトナム戦争時にアメリカ軍に攻撃されてしまったため、ほとんどが再建されたものです。再建されたものかどうかの見分け方は、古いレンガには苔が生えないけど、新しいレンガには苔が生えているそうです。遺跡観光は面白かった。ホイアンから車で1時間かけて行きました。高速道路は作っている最中のため、一部しか利用できませんでした。現地にレストランはありますが、衛生面でホイアンかダナンまで戻った方が良さそう。

I enjoyed seeing historical sites. My Son sanctuary is named as UNESCO heritage site. The temples are built by the Cham people during 3 to 7 centuries. The temples are correlated to Hinduism. The bricks are not cemented but using a tree raisins as a glue. Many of the temples were restored from the Vietnam war bombings. The entrance ticket VND 150,000 included a museum visit, Cham show, shuttle car ride to sites. It does get pretty crowded with tourists, hot and humid.

We had a wonderful half-day at the site. We did not have a tour-guide but it was not necessary for us as we have been already to several similar sanctuaries. There is a small museum near the entrance which is worth having a look. Although there is still a lot of conservation works going on some of the places are quite impressive. There are several options to buy water at the site so don't worry about the heat.
If you want to visit a area and learn about Champa culture. My Son Sanctuary it the place to come. Sadly distroyed by the American bombs .It still amazing contruction. It's like a mini Angkor Wat.
Apesar das bombas nesse lugar , há templos que permanecem em pé ! Uma ótima opção para quem procura conhecimento e história !

Well worth a visit but be prepared for large tourist groups if you don’t get there early. Also very hot and humid so take a hat and water as well as a guide who can explain the history and make sure you go around in a logical manner.We took a private car with guide from our Hoi An hotel leaving at 8:30 and we were back relaxing at the pool by 2:00 after cruising back up the river for the final part of the tour.Really good place to visit as part of your Hoi An leg of Vietnam tour

UNESCO heritage site not far from Hoi An. Miniature version of Angkor Wat. Many temples were however destroyed during the Vietnam war bombings as this site is very close to the Ho Chi Minh trail and Laos. The tour guides mention this every 2 or 3 minutes what a shame that the bombs ruined the site. I felt like being held responsible for the damage, just because I’m a foreigner...It is questionable how the site looked before the war, as the locals apparently used bricks from here as building material. There was a short musical performance in the visitors centre. Which was...unnecessary?
Wir waren im Rahmen einer Sunset Tour in My Son. Durch die Hintergrundinformationen war der Besuch der Anlage sehr interessant. Ohne Tour würde ich davon abraten.

The remains of the Champa kingdom, hidden back in a little valley, are a great example of brickwork and carving.  What has been uncovered to date--and not bombed by the Americans, sadly--is a religious complex well worth the trek out from Da Nang or Hoi An.  Strolling around the ruins, especially with a good guide, the detail of the craftsmanship is amazing.  There are many different temples open to the public, and a paved walking path leads among them.  Another striking part of the complex are the many bomb craters still visible, a sad reminder of what caused the destruction of some of these temples.  Unfortunately, the complex is being reconstructed, and the new buildings are losing some of that charm and all the authenticity of the old structures.  To get to the ruins, because they are in a small valley, one takes an electric cart from the visitor centre.  That is another downside to this place: you can see they are building this to accommodate mass tourism at some point--and that will overwhelm this complex.  Visit while it's still a bit off the beaten track.
美山遣址可以說是越南版的小吳哥，可以了解占婆的歷史，這裡真的很熱，小黑蚊也蠻多的，來這記得要擦防曬和防蚊液喔

The temples date from the 8th and 14th Centuries, some have been slightly restored and others are quite ruined. You’ll also see the bombing craters from the Vietnam War. About 6 main temples in all with smaller ruins which I passed by. Everyone went in the opposite direction to what was in the map which is the way to go! They also take you on electric cars to the temple area which is about 2km from the entrance. There are refreshments and WC in the middle area.

The sanctuary is hidden in the mountains and is very peaceful. We had the luck to have it almost to ourselves at 4 pm. An important detail you ought to know is the transport to the sanctuary itself - there is actually no bus that will take you directly there. Buses take you to the village called My Son situated 9 kms from the actual sanctuary, so you have to take a taxi or a Grab to go up, costing around 300K. The last bus going back is at 4 pm, and it has no fixed schedule, making it unreliable. At the sanctuary itself there is no public transport, and at times calling a taxi can be a challenge too. If you ride a scooter or can hire a car, it's a smart thing to do. Otherwise go early and get extra cash for a taxi!

I was really looking forward to coming here but with hindsight wish we’d gone even earlier in the morning- it was so hot!We arrived at 9am and spent 3 hours looking around the museum first to learn about the buildings, Cham people and archeology then proceeded to the ruins and finally the show of Cham culture.Unfortunately I didn’t enjoy it as much as I should have because of the heat. Go early!Luckily you can buy cold drinks up there.

We visited early in the day and I'm sure that was a good ploy. Having spent the night before at the My Son Heritage Resort and Spa, we took a car to the site around 7am. We had the place almost to ourselves, with it taking just under 2 hours to walk around the loop and wonder at the ruins (some of which are actually in decent shape) before returning to the hotel for breakfast. As we left, the crowds were starting to arrive. Very impressed at the availability of cold drinks across the site, at very reasonable prices (VND10-15k) for a bottle of water or a can of coke.
美山聖地距離峴港約一小時的車程有小吳哥窟之稱是個被聯合國列為世界文化遺址。他的歷史起源於占婆王朝，參觀這裡大約需要花半天的時間雖然沿途有許多樹蔭但還是很熱記得帶水及傘，也由於東南亞常為登革熱疫情建議做好相關防護措施。

If you're staying in Hoi An for more than a few days, My Son is definitely worth seeing. About a 50 minute drive away, they show ruins of the old Champa people. People compare this place to Angkor Wat - it's obviously a lot smaller but still worth a visit in its own right. To compare the two is silly. A good place to spend a few hours, but it is incredibly hot! 🥵We arrived at 2:30pm at it was perfect - hardly any tourists and we could get great photos with nobody else in them. Ignore all these nonsense about getting up at the crack of dawn to visit at opening. Tour guides all say the afternoon is by far the quietest. Just avoid 8am-1:30pm as it's busy!

前年度にアンコールワットに行っていたので、ミーソンはこじんまりとしていて、サクッと観光ができる遺跡で、夏場の暑い時期にはちょうどいいと思います。英語のガイドさんが、感情をこめていろいろ説明をしてくれたので、とても楽しく話を聞きました。ベトナム戦争の傷あとで、爆弾の跡が複数あり、ここを本拠地として構えていたので、狙われたと説明がありました。そのせいもあり、遺跡はかなり破壊されています。イタリア・イギリスの政府と共同で修復作業をしています。ツアーにはフランス人もいたのですが、遺跡の多くをフランス人が持って行ってしまって、返さないという話で、フランスの人も驚いていました。いろんな国の人がいるツアーでしたので、そこもとても面白かった。でも、暑かった。。。。
ベトナムのイメージというと、東南アジア圏でありながら中国の影響をほかのおなじ東南アジア諸国より強く受けているイメージですが、このミーソン遺跡はタイやカンボジアの遺跡に近い建築物で、文化のつながり・まざりあいを感じました。とても暑いです。

Perché la mattina è molto affollato e fa molto caldo. La zona è stata bombardata ed è indispensabile camminare sul percorso previsto perché la zina non è stata tutta smineralizzata. Il tempio più grande in mattoni rossi è luogo di studio sulle modalità adottate per la costruzione.L’architettura dimostra ingerenza sia della cultura del regno dei Champa che indiana e indonesiana. Il secondo tempio che abbiamo visitato è stato restaurato da archeologi italiani

Having been to Angkor Wat, the architectural sites are sightly different constructed with small bricks instead. Also, the ruins are already in pretty bad shape. For first timer, it is still an eye opener. Dress in light gear and be prepared to walk. We skipped quite a number of sites as the weather was too hot!

ベトナム中部を長らく支配していたチャンパー王国の遺跡です。中国文化圏ではなく、東南アジア文化圏に位置づけられる遺跡ですが、よっぽどベトナムの歴史に詳しい人でない限り、いろいろと説明してくださる現地のガイドさんと一緒に回るのがいいと思います。ツアーコーディネーターさんも、「個人でタクシーをチャーターしてくる人がいますが、説明を聞かないで回るのはもったいないと思います・・・」とおっしゃっていました。文字による解説がほとんどありません。またこの遺跡はベトナム戦争中、人が住んでいる村だと勘違いしたアメリカ軍が爆撃をしています。爆弾が落ちた場所には大きな穴が開いています。それも含めて学ぶことの多い遺跡だと思いました。

古代チャンパ王国の聖地だった遺跡です。未だにレンガを接着剤を使わず組み立てている方法や建物の厚みが上にいくほど暑くなっていく工法はわかっていないそうです。しかもヒンドゥー教の遺跡というのも珍しいと思います。（インドとベトナムが共同発掘してるようです）入り口から2kmほど電気自動車で運んでもらいそこからは歩きです。私たちが行った時はめちゃくちゃ暑い日でしたので遺跡を巡るのも一苦労しましたが、苦労しても見る価値のある遺跡だと思います。
A very interesting location comprise of rich and mixed heritage. A must visit tovisualise civilisation ruin. A colony that could not withstand the test of time.
這裡很曬很熱，比較適合一早前來。由峴港坐GRAB 約一個多小時。因為之前已到過吳哥窟，感覺和這兒很像，不過人流少很多，可以輕鬆拍照。這兒的規模也很小，大概2小時就遊覽完。
どこに行ってもそうですが、現地ガイドさんに説明をしてもらうと自分では分からない事がいっぱいあって勉強になります。同じように見える遺跡ですが、その時々に違う所からの影響を受けていて、それが建物の彫刻などに影響を与えています。

We did a small group tour to My Son Sanctuary by mini bus. Arrive at the entrance where tickets can be purchased. Open air buses transport tourists 2 km to the start of the trail. Toilets, drinks, snacks available at the start of the trail. The pathway is paved, well maintained and mostly shaded. However some areas near the temple involve steps and uneven ground that would be difficult for people with mobility issues. It’s still incredibly hot as it’s in a valley and there was no wind at all on the day we visited. You will sweat up a storm. Take a hat, water and a hand fan. There are only about 6 or 7 remaining temples, in various stages of being restored as they were bombed to bits by the Americans in the 1960s ( I am told). There are still many bomb craters right next to the temples. The university of Milan ( Italy) and Indian engineers are helping to restore the temples It was not too crowded when we were there and I managed to get photos unadorned by the many tourists who apparently MUST climb all over these sacred sites to take selfies. I’m not sure why there is that compulsion to be photographed ON the sacred monument rather than photographing it as part of its natural environment. I think allow about 2 hours at the site. There was a brief traditional musical and dance performance at 10 am while we were there, which was nice. Worth a visit at least once if you are staying in Hoi An or Danang.
One of the MUST travel spots in Da Nang. Thx to the sky! After big rain, sunshine came for us to have a chance to walk back and take good photos ^ ^

Quần thể di tích lịnh sử không lớn,tháp không đồ sộ,nhưng các hoa văn cổ đã rất tinh sảo rất thẩm mỹ và đẹp , cả quần thể được gìn giữ chăm sóc  sạch sẽ văn minh ,đáng tiếc vì bị chiến tranh làm hư hại nhiều quá .rất khâm phục kỹ thuật xây đưng của người Chăm xưa .

Breathtaking landscapes with a backdrop of modern and ancient history, best describes the My Son Temple. As a western tourist, I would recommend getting a tour guide and visiting in the afternoon (it's cooler, and less crowded) - a must for this attraction. The history of the area is astounding, going back thousands of years with the temples and modern history with the bomb craters. Well worth the admission fee for a step back in time.

ミーソン遺跡には、Grabをチャーターして行きました。片道1時間15分、往復で2時間30分、遺跡内には1時間30分程度いたので、トータル4時間で1,000,000VNDという価格でした。道も空いていましたし、時間からするとちょっと割高な価格なってしまったかもしれません。さて、ミーソン遺跡は、ベトナム中部クアンナム省にある古代チャンパ王国の聖なる遺跡で、1999年、ユネスコの世界遺産に登録されています。チケット買って(150,000VND)ゲートを入ると右手に展示の建物が見えてきます。そこに軽く立ち寄った後、道に沿って進むとグリーン色の電気カートが何台か駐車している場所につきます。ここで、このカートに必ず乗ってください。料金は無料です。遺跡に徒歩で向かうとかなり歩きます。というか、暑い中歩くには危険な距離だと思います。遺跡を見た後はカートを下ろされたところに帰りのカートが止まっているので、それに乗ってまた戻ってください。カートを降りたらいよいよ遺跡巡りです。順路を示す看板があるので、左右どちらから進んでも円を描くように戻ってきます。遺跡を見ながら写真を撮っていくと1周するのに少なくとも1時間はかかると思ってください。結構広いです。水分補給は必須です。だいぶ崩れている遺跡もありましたが、当時の雰囲気は私にも伝わりました。ダナン市内からは結構離れていますが、世界遺産好きな方はぜひ訪ねてみてください。

Booked this trip from our hotel. It is an interesting place with plenty of history. The guides explain about the place. You even see bomb craters here from American B 52's. The afternoon we went, the humidity was through the roof. Just looking at ourselves & others, you would think we were using water cannons on each other, we were dripping

ホイアン郊外にあるチャンパ王国の遺跡です。個人で行くには不便な場所なので、ホイアン市内から日帰りツアーを利用しました。市内から車で約１時間。駐車場から、少し歩き、電動カートに乗り、さらに徒歩で５分ほど進むと遺跡群が見えてきます。遺跡群はいくつかのブロックに分かれていますが、見学のメインはB〜Dブロックと呼ばれる一団になります。遺跡自体はベトナム戦争時にアメリカ軍の爆撃により破壊されており、今姿を見せているものは基本的には復元物になるそうです。それでも、ヒンドゥー教の神々の彫像が施された遺跡の姿は当時を忍ばせます。なお、遺跡の見学は暑さとの闘いです。山々に囲まれた窪地にある遺跡の中は、見学に訪れた日も気温が４０℃近くまで上がり、日差しも強く、湿度も高く、汗を吹き出すようにかいても、身体に熱がこもります。きちんと暑さ対策をし、飲料水を持参して水分補給しないと確実に熱中症に陥りますので、くれぐれもご注意ください。

A long journey from anywhere and a long way to go with the restorations, but enough to get a sense of a very ancient society. Most people skip the museum near the entrance but this is a big mistake as it gives a very good sense of what is there.Go very early to avoid both the hoards and the heat!

We visited here with a private guides and had a lovely day.My Son is about an hour’s drive from Hoi An and we got there mid morning. It was relatively busy but can get a lot busier later in the day. The weather was thankfully partly cloudy and only about 32°. Our driver and guide said it gets very warm because of its valley location so try choose a cooler day.We’ve seen some amazing sights on our holiday and this was great but didn’t quite live up to other UNESCO sites if you’ve been to Halong Bay and Hue Citadel. But it’s still a must-visit place and a great location in the mountains/forest.

Good to visit just by the way - if you travel south - north or opposite, e.g. Da Nang - Hoi An or back - just go there and this is max 2 hours. We took private car for 4 person family from Hoi An to Da Nang through My Son with ~ 2h stopover. Booked by hotel = 1M VND for good seven sitter suv.I'm not that much archeological passionate - so, just nice ancient ruins for me. On the pictures looks a little bit more amazing than in reality in my opinion :)

We hired a private car and left the hotel at 6:30am. 800VD return. Listened to advice re beating the heat and crowds. Good advice. We had a guide who was most informative. Ruins are being restored faithfully. Significant historical significance. Setting is beautiful. Short show at 9:15 also really entertaining. Definitely worth a trip, but suggest you avoid group tours if you can.

Рекомендую к посещению тем кто действительно любит исторические объекты.Очень интересное и необычное место мне почему-то напомнило Чьяпас в Мексике и даже архитектора похожа только здесь применен кирпич.на территории проходят представления (танцы) было интересно посмотреть. Жалко что комплекс как-то очень своеобразно восстанавливают на фото видно места восстановленных стен, да и вообще я не заметил какой либо активной  деятельности по восстановлению храмового комплекса.Обязательно берите с собой питьевую воду.

To be honest these temples do not compare with those of Angkor Wat and the Ayutthaya. However if you lower your expectations you will still enjoy these ruins and take some great photos. 65% of the temples were destroyed by the American bombs during the war and there are still bomb craters beside some temples. To avoid the heat and the crowds we did the 4:30am tour - which gets you to the entrance at 6am. Once inside, a tramcar will take you the 2km up to the ruins. The first group of ruins we went to were the largest and most picturesque, so try to take your time here. There is a cafe and toilets at the entrance and another at the tram return spot.

ダナンで手配した現地ツアーで訪れた世界遺産。宿泊したホテルから一時間程度。8ー13世紀にこの地にあったチャンパ王国の遺跡。ヒンドゥーのシヴァ神を祀ったレンガ造りの遺構を数多く見ることができる。一方でベトナム戦争時にベトナム解放軍が基地として利用していたらしく、アメリカの爆撃で崩壊が進行した様子も伺える。古代史と現代史の二つの雰囲気を感じ取れる不思議な空間であった。注意点は、とにかく暑い（6月訪問）ことと、意外と歩く距離が長いという点。現地でも入手可能だが、水分補給は必須です。

Once we arrived at My Son, we took a purpose provided road train, then what semed a longish walk to a café and souvenir market within the sanctuary.  It was hot and completely exposed:   midday and 31 degrees.  Then another walk along a slightly shaded road to what turned out to be a smallish, slightly over-grown cultural site with lots of crumbly and not-so-crumbly temples.  These 4th and 14th C Hindu temples were far more interesting to me than many of the over-restored, sanitised versions elsewhere.  I enjoyed it much more than expected, possibly because it was all accessible and each structure was different in some way.   I didn’t go on to the next element of the site but stayed and enjoyed the quietude, sounds and flowers around and about.  This is an UNESCO site. Pity so much was destroyed by US carpet bombing.
Lättillgängligt och väl anpassat för alla. Väldigt fin natur runtom ruiner av tempel och bombnedslag. Känns speciellt. Bra med caféer och restauranger i området men mat för bra priser.
In die tijd was My Son een belangrijk keizerrijk opgetrokken uit rode bakstenen. Tijdens de Vietnam oorlog is het complex verwoest door een bombardement van de Amerikanen.
Zo mooi, zo vredig en ook weer een stukje geschiedenis wat eigenlijk op een rondreis niet mag ontbreken.

We toured the My Son sanctuary early May and summer has officially begun. If you're visiting in the next couple of months, our guide said it will be even hotter.We brought umbrellas because a hat wont cut it. From the entrance, there's a small museum. You cross a bridge and ride a tram to a pitstop nearer the ruins that sells drinks/ice cream/food. There's about another 500m walk to the actual ruins. Nothing too strenuous but IT. WAS. SCORCHING and our clothes were drenched after about 30min.Wear light clothing and comfy shoes. There are trails and stairs and cemented pathways so it's not too hard. There are several ruins still being restored, so tents and construction materials are visible in some of our photos.It's sn interesting site. Get a guide to help explain the history since it's quite facinating.

宿泊先のホテルの目の前にレンタバイク屋さんがあったので、スクーターを借りてミーソン遺跡まで行ってみました。自己責任の下で借りましたが（1日8ドル、ガソリン別、パスポートなど身分証提出なし）ホーチミンやハノイとは違い田舎町だからバイク運転は怖くないだろうと思い借りてミーソン遺跡まで行きましたが、交通量による不慣れな怖さはありませんでしたが、のんびり走って片道2時間弱（行きは道に少し迷う）かかり時期的に暑かったのもあり、やはりツアーで行かれる方良いかなと思います。（欧米人ツーリストは結構バイクで来ていましたが）肝心のミーソン遺跡は思っていた以上整備されていて歴史を感じ良かったです。
If you like History dating back to the 4th century, then this is the place to go.A little hot an sweety, but well worth it, it been mostly destroyed by war, but they are rebuilding
ダナン、ホイアンから1時間程度で行ける遺跡スポット。近代的なダナンの町並みとは全く違う光景を見ることができます。ツアーでは伝統的なショーも楽しむことができます。一人で行くこともできますが、ツアーで行くほうが無難です。

We organized our own private tour.  It is a half hour journey from Hoi An.A nice place to visit in the misty morning.  Our guide had some knowledge of the area.  Enough to keep us entertained.Besides the ruins themselves, you are treated to local indigenous artists dance performances.The locals also sell various ethnic items.

My Son or ’beautiful mountain’ is a UNESCO world heritage site a one hour drive from Hoi An. We heard about it on our last visit to Vietnam but couldn’t fit in a trip to see it. That's why we were uniquely determined to pop by this time around. It is a sacred site built by the ancient Cham people. They are a distinct ethnographic group in Southeast Asia with origins in Indonesia. From the eighth to fifteenth centuries they built their Hindu shrines deep in the Vietnamese jungle. Each temple is dedicated to a different god including; Ganesh, Shiva and Vishnu. The temples lay hidden for nearly six centuries before their rediscovery by French explorers in 1895. Then began the not inconsiderable task of clearing a route to the site and making it available to visitors. Not only are the temples remarkable for their devotional decor but also their construction techniques. Perfect paper-smooth bricks were bonded by tree sap resin that made for flawless, seam-free walls that have stood the test of centuries of abuse and tribulations. This trauma included bombing from American B52s during the American War. And while a few payloads got through, happily the US pilots were terrible shots, and the damage was minimal. You’ve got to wonder about the mentality of people who think the destruction of ancient cultural artefacts is an effective way to win a war. It was a hot day, and the journey around the different temple sites was hard going. The physical effort needed to take good quality photos plus listening to the info’ from our guide was a challenging cocktail of competing tasks. While My Son is no Angkor Wat, it was worth a visit. It offered a fascinating insight into older Vietnamese history. It was also a welcome respite from what could have been a constant cycle of shopping, drinking and eating that characterises any trip to Hoi An.

展示館で説明を読むとより理解が深まると思います(英語/タイトルのみ日本語表記あり)カートを降りた後、ルート通りに歩くと1時間強。ルートと逆に行くとまず最初にメインの大きな遺跡があるので、時間がない人や沢山は見たくない人はここだけでいいかも。通常のカート降り場を超えて、そのメインの遺跡の近くまでカートに乗せてもらっている人もいました。カートにベルトで固定されていたので身体が不自由な方だと思いますが、そのような事も対応してくれるようです。さすが世界遺産。足元はだいぶ整備されていたのでロングスカートやショートパンツでも大丈夫だと思います。4月後半、現地の方の勧めで15時スタートにしました。最後の方は涼しく過ごせました。

I've never been to Angkor Wat or similar, so I can't compare to those, but I really enjoyed visiting this sanctuary. There is one particular site that is very well preserved and shows the grand nature of the temples. Unfortunately, a few of the sites have been reduced to rubble due to American bombing during the war which is a pity but the crater sites themselves are quite a thing to behold too. We had a guide with which helped explain some of the imagery found in the temple - I recommend that if possible. Be sure to bring water, hats and sunscreen - it gets crazy out here!

レンガでできているのに接着剤は使わずに建築したそうです。駐車場からカートに乗りますが、スピードが速いのでしっかり座っていないと落ちそうな勢いです。日影があまりないので、帽子と水、サングラスがあったほうがいいかもしれません。暑いですがサンダルだと遺跡部金は石がゴロゴロしていて歩きにくいです。途中で民族ショーをしているので休憩ポイントになります。余談ですが、ベトナム外務省の全面協力で、乃木坂46のMVに使われたそうです。

We hired a driver and car from Hoi An (USD$40) and got to My Son just before 8am. We did not rush our walk through the ruins (we went in the clockwise direction) and had many of the temple compounds to ourselves in the cool of the morning. We reached the main temple ruin before the crowds descended (we chose to miss the cultural show but could hear it). The signage was in Vietnamese and English so we were able to get a good understanding of the monuments and their placements. The museum is interesting too (we visited it on our way out). We returned to our driver at about 10.30, feeling unrushed and as if we had really explored the complex.

帽子、サングラス、水を忘れる方はいないと思いますが、それだけでは不十分です。他に持っていけば良かった、と感じたものをリストアップします。①大きめの傘…帽子とサングラスを装着した上で傘をさすと完璧です。ホテルに常備してある傘を借りると良いと思います。②マスク…舗道の熱気と土埃で喉がいがらっぽくなるので、使い捨てマスク持参をお勧めします。③髪をまとめるゴム…駐車場から遺跡群まで乗るカートが結構なスピードを出すので、髪の長い方は髪をまとめないと風で大変なことになりますよ💦サンダルで来られてる方も多かったのですが、遺跡が石で出来ているので、スニーカーの方が無難と思います。

This attraction is worth seeing if you are in the area.  It does get very hot so make sure you take water with you (you can buy it at the car park).However, BEWARE of the ticket booth staff that try to steal your money.  They tried it with me and nearly got away with it.I handed them a 500k note for the entry fee of my family (360k).  She then past me back 20k saying it was not enough.  I was a bit confused as I thought I had handed her 500k (500k and 20k notes look very similar).  As I had been distracted by my kids, I just took her word for it and then handed her a 500k note to cover the entry fee and got my change. Seeing what may have just happened, my wife then promptly informed me that this was a known scam and that I had just been fleeced of 500k.  As I stood to the side, I then counted the money in my wallet (as I had just made a withdraw from the ATM) and confirmed that I was indeed 500k short.  As I double checked the money in my wallet, the ticket booth lady kept on looking over.  Knowing that we had caught her out, she waved me back and said she had made a mistake and then handed my back my 500k, in exchange for the 20k notes she had earlier swapped for me.  Which is basically a confession that she had tried to steal from me.So enjoy your day, but be very careful of the thief selling the tickets.
Dommage que ce site ai été partiellement détruit par les bombardements americains dans les années 70. Malgré tout il y a encore de beaux restes.A voir  .

We went to see My Son in the early morning. We booked our tickets through get your guide and were a bit skeptical how it would work out but everything ended up very well.We got our mobile tickets right away and tour company even sent us an confirmations email. We were picked up early morning around 4.30 from our hotel and head it up to the My Son. Breakfast delivered was a small sandwich and banana.Only minus was that we were in the bus during the sunrise so we didn’t see it even we thought that we would get there before sun got up.The place is worth of seeing. I highly recommend to go there in the morning since it gets really hot after sunrise. Bring lots of water and some snacks with you since we were back at Hoi An around 10.00.

Beautiful and peaceful. Wonderful to see the ancient buildings and to be able to enter them and wander amongst the ruins. So sad to see the destruction caused by bombing.There is an interesting Vietnamese culture show prior to visiting the ruins.Wear insect repellent and make sure you have enough water as it is hot and humid. There is a fair bit of walking on these tours though part of it is covered by electric buggy.

We travelled to My Son with a private tour. We tried to make it an adventure for our children. They likely loved the bomb craters more than the ruins. I enjoyed the history behind the sanctuary. Please keep in mind that the war was not kind to My Son and 50% of the area was destroyed.

A good dose of history. Smaller than Angkor Wat but still worth to visit if you are in the area.As everyone is saying, it's really hot & humid there, so head covering, sun screen & fan is a must.They recently paved the walkway back from the site to the entrance & planted some trees on the sides - in a next few years they should provide good shades for otherwise very u comfortable walk back.
C'était bien joli. Nous avons eu la chance de ne pas avoir grand monde! Mais oula il faisait chaud. Apporter beaucoup d'eau! Beaucoup de ruines! Attention au gigantesque araignée, ça surprend x)
ダナンから約１時間程バスに揺られていきました。４月中旬でしたが、兎に角暑い。みんな汗びっしょりで、服がドボドボ。午前に出発して、１０時半でこの状態なので、午後はもっと酷いかと思います。タオルと汗拭きシートと水は必需品。遺跡はベトナム戦争時代にかなり破壊されてしまったようで、保存状態は良くないです。一応復元もされていますので、悪くはないかと。

A world UNESCO site so when in this area you should ensure you make a point of visiting Wear walking shoes, sun cream and bug spray and take your time to enjoy the work And history of the locationA guided tour will help understand the site more or pre visit the Cham museum in Danang prior Some walking involved but a historic experience

The remains are really quite inspiring as structures in themselves but without a guide I’m not sure you’d be able to really understand much about the significance of the site. We went at midday to avoid the crowds but my word it was hot - 38 degrees - so maybe do go earlier or later but definitely go.

great religious site of the ancient Champa Kingdom. Worth to see because of the Hindu temples which are unique in this region. This is a lot smaller and impressive than Angkor, so do this before you go to AW.

This place was fun to go to, and although I didn’t go with a tour I would recommend having some type of a guide to learn the history behind this place. It is a fairly long walk with some stairs to navigate, so be ready for that. My only complaint is that the shuttle path is paved almost all the way to the attraction, but they force you to get off at the souvenir shop and keep walking along the shuttle path! Whatttt?!? People were leaving with sleeping kids and crying kids (just a long walk in the hot sun) and to extend the shuttle along the already paved path would be a respectful courtesy to visitors... but nope, off at the souvenir shop hoping you buy.
遺跡の意味が分からないと、見ていてもわからなかったので、日本語ツアーで申し込み、正解でした。ベトナムの歴史を知るのによいと思います。ダナン、ホイアンからも近いので、ぜひお薦めします。ある程度、歩きやすい靴をお勧めします。虫よけ、日焼け止めも。

ホイアンでピックアップ、ミーソン遺跡と五行山を巡り、ダナンまで送迎があるプライベートツアーを手配。ミーソンは遺跡そのものを見に行く心づもりでいたが、到着直後にタイミングよくアプサラダンスのステージを鑑賞することができ、これが良かった。遺跡は歴史は感じられるのだが、保存状態が余り良くないので、どちらかといえば廃墟と言った方がしっくりくる感じ。朽ち果てた廃墟のようだが雰囲気はある。広い敷地内は、日陰が少ないので、夏季は暑さ対策が必要。

Set foot for a first time to see the ruins of My Son. Guide brief very clearly about the temples, mediation and the sculptures of the temples.  The area is currently under restoration. For ancient cultural lovers perhaps you explore. I went there around 4pm and the place is not crowded as tourist already left. The sun is also down (approching to sunset) and it was not hot to take some photos around. The nature air is clean and looking at the river while standing at the bridge brings inner peace. Good for nature lovers too.

J’avais pris le temps de lire les critiques sur TA. Ceux ci sont exacts et véridiques.Il y a énormément de touristes, il peut y faire très chaud, l’endroit est magnifique si l’on regarde avec des yeux de l’histoire Vietnamienne. Il est préférable d’y aller avec un guide pour l’histoire.Apportez vous de l’eau et il est possible d’y manger. Le temps d’attente pour les voitures électriques n’est pas si long. L’organisation y est bien.

This is a beautiful site surrounded by beautiful mountains (thus the name My Son...beautiful mountain). I leaned something new about the Champa kingdom and the influence they had. The sad fact is that the whole site was devastated by carpet bombing during the war. You can still see some of the bomb craters decades after. The dancing show is a bit hokey but still enjoyable.

Good walk if you want to spend a morning to enjoy fresh air and look some historical site. Don't compare with Angok Wat because this one is far too small in scale. The structure is quite intact. Some rectification work was done with new building material added so it looks a bit strange.A good introdcution if you want to have a look of this type of religious heritage.

Vi besøgte My Son på vores tur gennem Vietnam, og var på en halvdagstur med bådtur tilbage til den gamle by. Ruinerne var super flotte i solskinnet - perfekt til insta billeder hvis man er til sådan noget 😅- og super interessant at høre om byggestilen og religionen bag..
ミーソン遺跡。ダナンからタクシーでだいたい1時間くらい。50万ベトナムドンくらいかな。入り口入って少し歩いで電動バスに乗って遺跡の入り口まで。そこから歩いて回る形。所要時間は1、2時間くらいかな。友人が乃木坂ファンだったのでだいぶ楽しかったよう。かなり暑いので水は事前に入り口で買っておきましょう。
森林の中にあり，それほど遺跡周囲を触っていないので，森林浴をしながら遺跡を見れます．ベトナム戦争時代に爆弾で出来た穴に水がたまり，金魚が泳いでいたのですが，この今からは想像できないほどの悲劇がこの知であったことを実感できます．

Well worth a visit, this is possibly as close to actual jungle as you will get. A relic of the Champ tribal peoples who mixed Buddhist , Indian and Chinese influences and built what looks like a smaller version of the Angkor Wat using a method of brick laying still not fully understood, it owes it’s preserved sections to its relative isolation. Indeed,it looks very much like if the tourists stop coming , the jungle would take over pretty quickly. You walk a short distance from the entrance to a stop area where free electric shuttles whiz you along to the complex. There’s no set way to walk but most people follow the map and complete a circuit. The trick is to wait for the dancing to start in the specially constructed hut and visit the temples when they will be free of tourists. You miss the dancing but if you’ve got time to linger you can wait for the next performance. Bomb craters show where the Americans did their best to complete the destruction but the jungle will eventually do that.

A visit to My Son Sanctuary was one of the first stops on our tour of Vietnam so we had few expectations about what we'd see. On the whole it was worth the 1.5 hour bus journey to get there. The sanctuary was very heavily bombed durng the Vietnam/American war and many B52 bomb craters are still visible. Despite the fact that large parts of the site and buildings were destroyed or damaged enough remains to get an impression of what it was like.

Username: Hue Private Car & Airport Transfer – Hue
Comment: Địa điểm rất cổ kính, lâu đời và hoang sơ , dấu vết thời gian hàng ngàn năm vãn còn tồn tại, khách hàng của tôi rất thích khi đến đây
150 000 VDN per person is really god price for such nice place.Electric car will take you close from gate which is nice touch.Sure go on dance show it is beautiful.

We came here as part of an organised tour in December 2024. The entrance complex has some toilets alongside the ticket hall. There was plenty of parking for cars and buses of all sizes. I've added a photo of the map at the entrance to give you an idea of the size of the complex.You walk for a few hundred metres past the museum (more on that below) to the stop where you get into an oversized, electric golf buggy. These then whip you up the road to where the main ruins are.It's a former site of Hindu temples, so you'll see a lot of phallic symbols, amongst Sanskrit-like font and Hindu gods. You're free to wander into the different buildings - it was busy-ish when we were there, so we did have to wait to go inside some parts. There is a daily performance of culture dance etc. - we used this to view the first site whilst it was a bit quieter! It had rained earlier that morning, so some of the paths / stones were a bit slippery - something to bear in mind. Someone in our group did slip and hurt their ankle, so please take care and wear good footwear. There are some signs in English, but it's best to have a guide with you to explain the background and significance of the different sites / buildings.The first site is the best preserved, with some restoration happening. The second is more ruined, but was probably my favourite as you could see more of what it would have looked like. Towards the end of the tour, you'll see some standalone buildings that are surrounded by pits from bomb craters during the war. You then walk back down a boardwalk / path back to jump on the electric buggy and head down the hill - we spent about 2 hours at the site itself - you could be quicker if you wanted to.We then visited the museum near the entrance at the end. This explains a bit of the history of the site, information about the excavations etc and is worth a look.

We drove from Hoi An for less than an hour to this beautiful place. The complex is well maintained and you can look at the history of this place in advance after the entrance, from 150000 VND, in a beautiful museum. Afterwards you will be taken by electric car near the ruins. The paths and bridges are very easy to walk and the atmosphere in this area with the temple ruins is mystical and very beautiful. We really liked it. There are also some options for eating and drinking. Back we take the electric car to a bridge, which is near the exit. We stayed about 11/2 hours in this facility.

UNESCO WORLD HERITAGE SITE .They are ruins for the most part anyway. ( are already what restorations happen )The park has good facilities and when we were there in late afternoon very quiet.There are many trees and the views are beautiful.A shuttle runs from the entrance to the actual site.Definitely worth a visit.Did you find this info useful , please let me know with a 👍,Thanks in advance 🇧🇪

Fantastic !!. Group of 4 separate hindu brick temple enclosures from the Bayan period. Beautiful kept UNESCO site with transport close to the sites by Golf Cars from the entrance. 5 euro admission. Highly recommended. 2-2.5 hours to get round. There is also a 15 mins performance in the theatre of dance and music. Great day out, about 1 hour from Hoi an old quarter by car.

My Son is an excellent place to visit. I would also highly recommend having a guide with you (or using the audio guides as a minimum) or you won’t know what you’re looking at. My Son is just over an hours drive outside Hoi An and you are initially dropped at a ticket office with toilets, a cafe and shop. You pay here (or your guide does) and you take a small complimentary buggy (seats 14) about 2km to the main entrance of the site. Inside the site are multiple places to sit and cool down, places to buy drinks (and gifts) and one set of toilets. But none of these ruin the ambience in any way, as they are subtle. Now the site itself - most tours seemed to be 4 hours in length (including transfer to/from Hoi An). That’s just not long enough to see the site. We paid a very small amount for an extra hour on our tour and it was well worth it. We got to visit each part of the site which wouldn’t be possible in the 1hr30 a normal tour gives you. So pay for an extra hour or you’ll be rushing and will miss out. In addition to the sites, there is a short 15 minute local dance performance at set times under a shelter. We had low expectations and planned to use the time to cool down and have a drink. We were pleasantly surprised and we enjoyed it. They are very talented performers so credit where credits due. Summary - My Son is definitely worth a visit; take a guide to show you around; and pay for the extra hour in the site.

Vietnam has several outstanding sightseeing places in this one is definitely at the top. I think this should be one of the seven wonders of the world my tour guide.Hang maybe feel like I was living in the past with everything that was happening. I felt part of the history when she was explaining to me in detail. Then once I’ve seen a show, everything was wonderfully explain to the part where I relieved I was there then the boat ride going to the luncheon maybe even feel like I was a king there I would recommend this visit to everybody this is a once in a lifetime adventure, and I think it should be one of the seven wonders of the world
Ohromující ruiny dávných chrámů zasvěcené králům. Procházení se mezi nimi je samo o sobě zážitek s mírně tajuplnou atmosférou (pokud se dokážete odprostit od davů turistů).

Thánh địa Mỹ Sơn là nơi lý tưởng cho những ai yêu thích lịch sử. Khu di tích bao quanh bởi núi rừng. Các đền tháp Chăm Pa với kiến trúc độc đáo, gạch nung xếp khít khiến tôi rất ấn tượng. Đây là điểm đến đáng để ghé thăm khi đến Quảng Nam

So worth it. They have electric cars that take you to the ruins. Catch the show if you can. Easy and flat to walk aroundPlenty of places to stop and test and buy drinks. Older than Ankgor Watt in Cambodia.

The ancient remains of the My Son Cham temples nestle in the green forest/jungle about an hour from Hoi An.  Already in ruins, the buildings suffered further destruction during the American War, but some have been partially reconstructed.  Wong was our excellent guide (booked through Handspan) providing plenty of useful information to help us better understand the site. Come early (the site opens at 5am) if you can to avoid the crowds and the heat.  There are electric shuttle buses covering the distance between the museum and the ruins, and cafes for cooling drinks.  The museum has lots of interesting information about the history and restoration of the site.
What a privilege to see a UNESCO World Heritage site. Lovely old temples, even some archaeologists busy excavating another temple.
Một trong những khu di tích cổ và đẹp nhất mình từng tham quan. Cảnh quan tuyệt vời, kết hợp giữa thiên nhiên và kiến trúc cổ xưa. Rất nhiều thông tin thú vị về nền văn hóa Chăm.

This is an impressive site, especially for those interested in archaeology or who enjoy walking in nature. The site is not very large, and a leisurely visit lasts no more than three hours. I recommend arriving early in the morning to avoid excessive heat and humidity, and to take water with you. There are a couple of small shops selling cold drinks at reasonable prices if you wish to purchase some. You will need a hat and sunscreen because the ruins are all in the sun. I also recommend wearing light, closed-toe shoes, not because the paths are difficult, but because of the dust. With a guide to explain things, we would probably have understood a lot more about the history of the place, but it was also nice to explore freely on our own.

We adored this place.  Whilst we are not religious, you could feel the spirituality around you.   If you are into historical relics and artifacts then this is definitely the place to come.   Very calm and tranquil, it’s a must see on any traveler’s ‘to do’ list.   Hint:  do the sanctuary on your own without a tour guide.    This will give you a lot more time to really breathe it all in without the usual rush rush rush.

These impressively designed tower temples and monuments are attributed to the Champa Kingdom and built between the 4th C–13th C. This dramatic, strategic My Son site was their capital, hidden by a ring of mountains and easy to defend. I was in complete awe and understand why its unique history and culture gave rise to an UNESCO WHS designation in 1999. The monuments here have carvings of Hindu mythical figures and dedicated to the mythical sacred Mount Meru. It is monumental site giving you a rare glimpse into the Champa Kingdom.  There are many stories associated with this site and according to the local guide would take weeks to describe in full. I only know a couple and am very glad I do....
It's amazing. I think some people wish it were a little far away, let alone raining. But the rainy ruins are also tasteful and you will think it is wonderful. Worth a look.

Visiting My Son Sanctuary was like stepping into a lost world. Tucked away in a lush green valley, these ancient Hindu temples—built by the Champa Kingdom—felt both peaceful and mysterious. Walking through the ruins, with jungle sounds all around, you really get a sense of the history and spiritual weight the place once held.Even though some of the site was damaged during the war, what’s left is stunning—ornate carvings, weathered bricks, and beautiful stone sculptures, all dedicated to Shiva. It’s wild to think how advanced their architecture was so many centuries ago.It’s about an hour and a half from Da Nang, but totally worth the trip. If you’re into history, ancient civilizations, or just love exploring hidden gems, My Son is a must-see. Just bring water—it gets hot out there!

My family and I came here to visit the beautiful Champs historical national park. We were enjoyed viewing many beautiful ancient concrete Champs buildings. We had a great time visiting this national park. The staffs were very helpful and professional. They do have a restaurant where you can enjoy dining champs authentic foods and gift shop inside the national park.

The Cham people of the Champa Kingdom - now a minority group in Vietnam - ruled the area with the temples dating back to the 4th to 13th centuries.  They were Hindu temples with Shiva being much revered.  Indian archaeologists were instrumental in revealing some of the temples ruins and helping to conserve them.  The Italian government and Germany (Daimler Benz) have also helped with conservation work.It's a UNESCO World Heritage Site and particularly interesting because most of the temples were built from brick, not stone.  Made from local clay and with resin added.  The plant resin was also used to 'stick' the bricks together like mortar.There is walking involved and some of the areas possibly aren't too good for people with mobility issues.Well worth a visit but best done with a guide who can give history and context.  It makes all the difference.

One of the highlights for me was visiting My Son.  I was really fortunate to meet a lovely Australian couple who had their own local tour guide and they invited me to join them. My Son is a special place but I can not stress enough the importance of having a local tour guide as they can give context, history and a real richness to the tour. The performance was also really good but I would suggest getting a seat early as it is very crowded and can be a bit hard to see. Loved this trip & thanks heaps Steve, Lorraine & tour guide Sing.
Great place to visit and sniff some ancient culture, nice walk amongst ruins and well kept grounds. Restaurant is so-so but it's the only place around.

Instead of arriving in early morning, I reached at 10:45. Most people already entered the scene and I needed not to queue for the shuttle vehicle. After viewing the art performance show starting at 11:15, I visited the sites from where a lot of people had gone. I left at 12:30 and shuttle vehicle was ready at stop.

Interesting place to see with beautiful views of the mountains around. I'm glad history is being preserved. Some arrows pointing direction of visit would be useful, but it's still ok to wonder around.

The Sanctuary, also known as the “Little Anguita of Vietnam,” is the site of Vietnam’s largest ancient monastery, built between the fourth and fourteenth centuries, and once had 70 temples. The kingdom is influenced by the Indian culture, worshipping the Brahman religion and worshipping the wet temple, the most famous of which is the Temple of the Wet Temple. The entrance fee for the Sanctuary ticket is 150K Vietnamese Shields/chip (about Taiwanese dollars 200 yuan), wearing some bright clothes, the photographer will be more prominent.

Amazing complex beautifully kept but with ruins they don’t know how to restore as it is the only complex of its time dedicated to Shiva.  Built across many eras but the earliest is 6th century. You can still see the craters from the bombing during the Vietnam war the high destroyed much of the complex.

You need some time for the activity My Son. At first it is a bit remote from HUe or Da Nang, depending on where you reside. Once there, you first go to the small museum and find out.Afterwards you walk a bit and then take an electric bus to the actual ruins.  Before or after you can attend a free dance performanceThe many bomb craters on the site are impressive in addition to the ruins and temples.

Tuan (Brian) took us on a fabulous trip to My Son Sanctuary- he is friendly, knowledgable and very easy to spend time with.  He clearly gave us information about where we were and the history- we highly recommend Tuan as a guide - it has been a great day!
Shouldn’t be missed! It’s amazing - unluckily I wasn’t able to cover the entire area as it was drizzling- if you want to understand the ruins, it’s advisable to take a guide.

We decided to spend an afternoon visiting the site. We hired a driver with a vehicle and created an ad hoc excursion with our timetable. Departure Da Nang and in about u hour and twenty we reached our destination. I took the tickets on the spot and followed the directions. These temples are built with clay bricks and and sandstone blocks. They had reached such a level of mastery that they could engrave and decorate them. Next to the site is wild jungle, the site is visible even in the rain the main walkways are on concrete or brick although in some stretches there is mud but in Vietnam the rain is at home. In a 2 or 3 hours the site is visited. It is advisable to read something before visiting the site.There are as in other attractions, bathrooms and dining options.
This place is fantastic, I would recommend visiting around midday as all the morning tourists who need to visit hoi an same day leave. Was very few people!

I went on tour, so I observed it while listening to the explanation slowly for about two hours. The site was declared a World Heritage Site in 1999 after the remains of King Champa. Unfortunately, however, the site was apparently devastated by American bombing as the Liberation Army of North Vietnam was based there during the Vietnam War. It is now being repaired and preserved little by little. This is one of the ruins I would love to see.

Nestled amidst the lush green hills of the Trường Sơn mountain range, My Son Sanctuary stands as a breathtaking testament to the ingenuity and spirituality of the ancient Champa civilization. Recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999, this sacred site is a must-visit for anyone eager to explore the deep cultural and historical roots of Vietnam.My Son enchants visitors with its collection of over 70 well-preserved temples and towers, constructed between the 4th and 13th centuries. These structures, built entirely from baked bricks and adorned with intricate carvings, showcase the extraordinary craftsmanship of the Champa people. Each tower is dedicated to Hindu deities, especially Shiva, and narrates stories of devotion, mythology, and artistic excellence through its detailed sculptures and motifs. Despite the ravages of time and war, the site’s majestic ruins evoke a sense of awe and reverence.Beyond its architectural wonders, My Son offers a serene retreat into nature. Surrounded by dense jungle and tranquil streams, the sanctuary provides an opportunity to connect with both history and the natural world. The peaceful ambiance invites reflection and a deeper appreciation of the Champa legacy. Visitors may also enjoy traditional Cham dance performances, a vibrant showcase of the community’s enduring cultural heritage.A visit to My Son Sanctuary is not just a journey into Vietnam’s past but a chance to witness the enduring dialogue between ancient traditions and the present. Whether you’re a history enthusiast, a culture seeker, or simply a traveler in search of serenity, My Son promises an unforgettable experience.Let My Son tell its story of resilience and grandeur – a story that continues to inspire generations.​

Well worth the journey from Hoi An. Very interesting learning about the Champa Kingdom.  Entrance fee very reasonable. Informative information boards throughout the site. Good that the entrance and car park was some distance from the site which added to its overall feeling of tranquility and serenity. The free shuttle 'buggies' to and from the car park enhanced the experience of going 'into' the jungle to the site. Well surfaced paths around the site.We hired a motorbike from Hoi An to travel to My Son. We would reccomend this but only if you have a uk m/bike licence and experience of riding m/cycles. Good views of surrounding countryside and 'feel' of the area. The parking at the site is very cheap, well organised and secure.

We went there as a group; and had a day of heavy rain. Beautiful site, where history is so present. The ancient Sanctuaries, but also the craters, remains of the bombs. Be careful, on the paths, the edges are slippery with rain! We spent a whole morning there, then stopped for soup, just before the exit.  I would recommend both the walk and the soup;-)
I am not sure why this wasn’t on the top of things to do in Hoi An.The temples predate Ankor Watt, not many, but well worth the visit to learn about a previous culture

Underrated and not to be missed by lovers of Asian history.  Hard to believe that people have lived here since the 2nd century, have experienced war and peace, and the ruins survive to tell their story.  So much more to learn from this tranquil location about 45 minutes drive from Hoi An.  Go early to avoid the crowds and experience the sounds of the surrounding jungle.

This place is amazing. A lot of history and stories are told through the ruins of the temple.I visited there in the morning and walked through all the sites. The weather was nice and clear which added beauty to the temples.

Had a great guide who told us a lot about the ruins, ancient and more up to date history. The pathways are well thought out so you get to see everything as well as admire the natural beauty of the place. It’s a wonderful experience.Make sure you have a hat and water as it gets very hot!!! And get there early to avoid an influx of coach tours around 10am!!!
A ticket for 1 person costs VND 150,000. There's a free electric car on the property. There's also a free theater schedule. You're gonna love it!
Chickytrung was a awesome tour guide, very knowledgeable and so much fun, highly recommend both going to this attraction and with this company

Amazing view of the ruins! There is a shuttle bus which runs frequently and takes you directly to the site, so you don’t have to walk all the way. There are also wonderful shows in the main hall at certain times during the day, so you should definitely time well to enjoy those shows!

The Hindu ruins are stunning, surrounded by green mountains. The guide explained fascinating details about the Champa civilization. The mystical atmosphere of the place creates a very romantic atmosphere. We recommend carrying repellent and water, it is very hot!

We had a great tour of the My Son Sanctuary with our guide, Chicky. He was really informative about the history and culture of the sanctuary and its people. And Chicky was great fun and interesting on the way to and from the sanctuary, with great insights into local culture.Highly recommended.
This archaeological site represents a moment in the history of the country still preserved with good reconstructions. To see the ethnic dance.
The site may seem of little interest compared to the rest, but it has the merit of being relaxing and being able to counterbalance the hysteria of other mass tourism sites.

In the afternoon, in August is not the best of life, a large parking lot, buses churn out waves of tourists, I wonder if it is worth facing heat and humidity. We start, a small museum to understand this civilization. The place however is fascinating, ancient ruins of temples immersed in the jungle, the heat does not help, but it was worth it.

We had read many luke-warm reviews about My Son and were a little reluctant to go. However, our hotel put us on to Dung (Mr Funny Power) at An Phu Tour Service (0903559441) and it was a great move. He was a brilliant guide who made the whole place come alive for us. He is extremely knowledgeable about the sanctuary having previously worked there, and told us all about it with great passion and power. Further to that, on the way to and from My Son we bombarded him with questions about life in Vietnam and came away with a much greater understanding of the country. What could have been a fairly ordinary experience turned out to be a great day - all because of our guide.

I'd recommend being there right at opening time of 6am , beat the buses and crowds and enjoy the tranquility of the site and in a cooler time of day. We enjoyed experiencing it without a guide and utilised the app if we needed more information. Nice to go at your own pace without the time setting if a bus or guide. Beautiful abundance of butterflies and birdlife, and amazing water Lillie's. Lovely meal at the cafe within the site, helpful staff dishing up authentic local dishes.

Très belle découverte avec l’audio guide, sinon ça aurait été compliqué.Arrivez avant 9h lorsque les bus de touristes débarquent.Intéressant de savoir qu’une civilisation aujourd’hui disparue était de religion hindouiste alors qu’elle se situait au centre du Vietnam actuel. Tant de choses restent mystérieuses…

Very interesting history and well preserved site despite so many years and what has happened in its history just after the bombings. 100% recommended visit consider going type half day, there are fewer people and that makes a visit more pleasant

This UNESCO world heritage site is a must visit if you are in close proximity to it. Try to get there by 9am as the heat and humidity can be stifling. Not to mention the hordes of tourists. In order to truly appreciate the history take the services of one of the guides.

We visited My Son by private transport enroute from Danang to Hoi An, spending three hours there. We planned on a midday arrival to avoid the bus tour timings and this worked well with the morning tours departing as we arrived and the afternoon tours behind us while we were there. In March it was quite warm but not horribly so.Once you enter there is a walk past the Museum and over a bridge to a spot where you can take a bus ride about 2 km to the cafe and information centre. You can also engage a guide here. From here there is a wide path which circles the area and bordered by gardens and seating which circles the whole area. We took the left route past the smaller sites first. On the otherside towards the end there was another cafe and toilets where we could take a break and reflect on this experience before walking the last part and boarding the bus back to the bridge.While the monuments are not as many or on the scale as similar ones in Cambodia - [no] thanks to the American bombing, My Son is nevertheless still a significant and atmospheric site. On reflection, I would visit again should I get the chance.Many of the ruins are being repaired with assistance from India as evidenced by scaffolding. There are still visible bomb craters and the advice is to keep on the paths and worn tracks.

We hired a driver for this morning trip to My Son which took about 1 hour 10 mins from Hoian beach hotel. We arrived at 8.45am and ahead of the bus trips. An electric car took us closer to the site where we arranged a guide for 100k dong. He walked us to 2 of the temple complexes. It was worth the general and specific info he gave. This lasted about 30 mins and for me could have been longer but worth the money paid. Stunning scenery and well worth the effort to get there.

Have holidayed in Hoi An many times but this was first my  visit to My Son. Didn’t take a tour but had an enjoyable ride to there through rice fields etc on my rented scooter. Undercover parking near to the ticket office VND 5k. Just inside the entrance there is a small museum, which was quite good, especially if you can eaves drop on some of the tour guides like I did. From there, there are electric cars which take you about 2km to not far from the temple complex itself. I was lucky to arrive just in time to see a traditional performance by some girl dancers and a trumpet (more like a flute) player. I have read a previous review saying to skip this, but totally disagree. It was a highlight; the girls were spectacular both in their costumes and dancing, and the “trumpet”  guy blew my, and everyone else’s, socks off. The Temple complex itself was a mixture of partly destroyed and intact structures, all amazingly built and great to look and marvel at. Each cluster was joined by paved paths making it easy to go from one to the other.In summary - so pleased I finally visited My Son and, the traditional show being one of the highlights.

We booked a car from our Hotel in Hoi An, and visited without a guide, plenty of info, very serene place if you arrive before 8 am before the larger tours, lots of great Hindu temples and B52 craters, all finished off with a local show, very good all round.

We went by car, arranged by our hotel. We left at 1pm and arrived before 2pm. The car park was almost empty when we got there.
