Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was an eye-opening experience for our family. The intricate network of secret entrances and exits showcased brilliant military strategy. We learned so much about the war history and the resilience of those who lived there. A must-see for anyone interested in history! 👍

Second time I have visited here, I was interested to hear the history and see how they lived in the tunnelsA eye opener to see the primitive traps they made amazing Great to crawl around the tunnels with a guide lots of exits if u need to get out well worth doingThe gun range at the end is cool was able to shoot a few rounds which was fun. Well worth the visit thank you
We booked our tour with Asiana Link travel and our tour guide was Slim Jim. The tour was very enjoyable, Slim Jim was so knowledgable and attentive! Would highly recommend him and Asiana Link Travel
We did the group of -9 and it was fantastic. Our guide was extremely knowledgeable and made the experience well worth it. Highly recommend

What an amazing tour..  we has Jackie as our tour guide who was absolutely amazing. He really made the tour. Thanks so much and we'll definitely be recommending the tour to others. Regards the Swans from Australia.

Very good and beautiful visit where we learn a lot of things and we can see reality in the face. Small downside for us who don't speak English but we understand that it's not easy to understand everything. But we had a very nice guide who still tried to explain something to us. I recommend XUYEN Thank you very much ❤️

Our guide was a very fun guide. He was very knowledgeable about the tunnels.  He was also a very fun and funny guide.  He made a sad time of history very informative.  Sorry i do t remember our guide name, i need to write these things much sooner….

Cu Chi Tunnels, very disappointing! Sad that they have developed this to its full potential. Given the number of visitors, and the history, there should be a lot more to see. Luckily the Mekong Delta part of the tour made up fir it in some respect. My view, skip the tunnels and do a full day Mekong Delta

Our tour guide Larry was very knowledgeable, very friendly .He made this tour not only easy, entertaining but also very informative. With this tour we got to know little history and culture of Vietnam. We saw historical places, equipments, Statues during the tour. The tunnel part was really great. We had a small group of 11 people and finished the tour and all activities on time. I would like to thank Kim travel and Larry for making & organizing the tour very effectively.

Tiny tunnels, booby traps everywhere – it's remarkable what they endured! A word to the wise: if you're tall, prepare to crouch. And if you hate tight spaces, maybe just admire the historic site from above ground.

This tour was unreal. We learnt so much, specifically due to the knowledge of our tour guide Tuan who fought in the war and could give us insight. Tuan and our driver Mr. Kim made our experience amazing. The bus is incredibly comfortable, they give you water, and take you to a lovely lunch spot for the morning tour. You get to explore a small bit of the tunnels (they’re VERY small btw!!) and see some of the contraptions they created in the war. So worth the trip, and a great tour if you have limited time and can’t go for a whole day.

Our tour of the Cu Chi Tunnels was both educational and moving. This remarkable underground network is a testament to the ingenuity and resilience of the Vietnamese people. An authentic experience steeped in history, not to be missed during a stay in Ho Chi Minh City.
Everyone who visits Vietnam should see this place and absorb a bit of Vietnamese history. Authentic tunnels from the period of the war in Vietnam between the years 1955-1975.

Our Vietnamese guide took us to visit these tunnels and part of the area that was full of Vietnamese hideouts.The place is outside the center and reachable by car. I don't know if public transport arrives. I can't provide the cost of the ticket as it was already included in the tour for us, but I think it was around $10.Unfortunately, in this place the meaning that this place had for years has been lost, full of suffering and broken lives, whether they were Americans or Vietnamese.It seems like a place for fun, perhaps perceived as such also because the local guides talk about their nation's victory over the Americans.You can visit the tunnels and some trapdoors where you can hide for a video or a photo. There are somewhat grotesque reproductions of how bombs were made, with rather poorly made dummies. The interesting thing was a lady who prepared sheets of rice by spreading them out in the air to dry.The saddest part, however, was the target shooting with firearms, where for a fee you could use blank weapons and shoot at targets.It would certainly be worth visiting with greater respect.

Very sobering reminder of the atrocities of war, in my lifetime. All six of us left asking WHY, and somewhat sad. That said wouldn’t have missed it. Do some homework on the Vietnam war before you go, especially this area.

Enjoyed the whole trip there, learned a lot about the history of Vietnamese war, and how the people in the past suffered from the war. Too bad I had an injured left knee, not able to experience to go down to the tunnel. Will definitely revisit to enter into the tunnel.

Very well worked visit with a mix of video information, display of traps and VC tactics, captured tank, shooting range and, of course, various tunnels to see or go down.Learning of the extent and complexity of the tunnels was fascinating - they were so ingenious.  I was very much looking forward to then going in one for myself...and I did...only to feel a panic attack coming on!  Not something that has bothered me before but be aware that the space is very tight (you're almost crawling on your stomach).  Although what did for me was the fact there was a large step to drop down just as the space narrowed and got really dark and I couldn't cope with that combination.  No matter though as at this point you can back up and get out again, which I did.  Don't try to carry on and then get stuck!I had thought a soundtrack of gunfire was being played to give a war atmosphere but actually there is a range you can buy bullets for and do some rifle shooting.  The gift shop is also well stocked with good guide books and nice nik-naks.
Hien was our guide for a great tour of the Cu Chi tunnel system.  He was very knowledgeable of the area and provided a young person’s perspective of life in Saigon as well.  Thoroughly enjoyable!

We were impressed by the Cu Chi tunnels! It's amazing how the Viet Cong created this secret communication system. We even got to crawl through a section of the tunnel (not for the claustrophobic!). A fascinating and thought-provoking visit.

This place really displays the resilience and ingenuity of the Viet Cong during the Vietnam War.  They suffered and the Americans suffered - what a shame and waste this war truly was.  Come here to see just snippets of the amazing 125 mile long tunnel network and a Viet Cong way of life during the war.  Unforgettable experience!
Mary was great helped us with a great tour in the tunnels giving a description and overall knowledge of the whole area there
Highly recommendAwesome day out with lots of great history Ele was a great tour guideThank you Ele well done Ele was informative and loads of fun
Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was a sobering reminder of the war's impact. The resilience and ingenuity displayed there were truly humbling.

A great insight to the Vietnam war where you get to experience the hidden underground tunnels which were used by the Vietnamese army whilst agent orange was sprayed across the terrain. We were also accompanied by a very experienced guide, (Mary) who provided us an in depth history of the war.
On the way south, we passed this famous tunnel. Definitely a must see! It's unimaginable how the Vietnamese fought against America back then. They deserve our utmost respect.

The remarkably well-preserved tunnels and the ingenious traps designed to thwart enemies illustrated the sheer resourcefulness and resilience of the Vietnamese people during the war. The sprawling network of underground tunnels paints a vivid picture of the challenges faced by soldiers. This site is a must-visit for anyone seeking a deeper understanding of the Vietnam War's history and the lengths to which people will go in the name of defense.

We joined a small group to visit the tunnels.  We knew it would be emotional as my husband served in the navy during the war.  12 years ago he became paralysed from a tumour on his spine…thanks to agent orange.  Our guide first took us to a handicrafts commune for adult ‘babies’ of agent orange..  he used the exact words my husband has used repeatedly.  ‘We washed in the water, we drank the water, we cooked in the water’.  We met these artists and the guide explained why my husband uses an electric scooter.  There was much hugging and many tears and many thanks.  We were glad to help by purchasing some of their art.  When then went to the tunnels which were sobering.  Man’s inhumanity to man is indescribable….from both sides….and I think we should all face these atrocities in the hope they won’t happen again.  This tour affected me far more than I expected.  Do it!

This was such an interesting place to visit. It was fascinating and horrifying all at the same time. Our guide Phat was really informative and gave us a real feel of the time and hardships. It makes you realise how much you really don't know and understand about what was happening, in our lifetime. Definite a must see. Gives you so much food for thought.

The intricate tunnel network, with its hidden air vents, hospitals, and traps for enemies, demonstrated the ingenuity and resilience of the Vietnamese fighters. This experience provided a powerful perspective on the realities of the Vietnam War and the sacrifices made.

We were lucky enough to have an excellent, knowledgeable guide. If you dont like small spaces, going into the tunnel might not be for you. Definitely worth seeing, and depending on the person, can also be a somewhat emotional experience when you imagine what happened during the war.

We were amazed by the incredible network of underground tunnels the Viet Cong used during the war. It's mind-boggling how they lived, fought, and even had hospitals and weapon storage down there. The hidden entrances were so cleverly disguised! This tour definitely gave us a deeper appreciation for the resilience and ingenuity of the Vietnamese people. A must-see if you're interested in history or want a truly unique adventure!

I was really looking forward to this, but it was by some way the worst tour we had from our SE Asian cruise.  Yes, there are some fascinating insights about how the Viet Kong fought, lived, and emerged victorious against far superior American weaponry, but it could and should be done so much better.  It wasn't helped that we had an awful guide, but the the displays they had were very amateurish.  The place was also utterly ruined by a firing range where visitors were able to fire some of the guns used in the war.  How tasteless is that?  It is also exceptionally loud and is deafening when in the shop / cafe area.  No doubt the guides get a cut from those who want to play with the guns that caused so much suffering.  It is touristy when it should be a sobering experience.  And tunnels?  you get to see about 10 metres.

The tunnels provide powerful evidence of the brutality of the Vietnamese war….and the ingenuity of the people to survive in horrible conditions. While not forgetting the past the country has moved on in amazingly positive ways.

We visited the Cu Chi tunnels and we were both impressed and a little claustrophobic! It's crazy to see this huge underground network that the Viet Cong built. We discovered the ingenious traps used against the French army, and it really highlights their resilience. If you want to dive into the history of Vietnam in an original way, we recommend the tunnels!

Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was an eye-opening experience.  We learned about the intricate communication systems and hidden air vents used by the Viet Cong during their resistance against French colonialism. It was fascinating to see the resourcefulness and determination displayed in such harsh conditions. A must-see for those wanting a deeper understanding of the Vietnam War.
always  wanted ot go and glad I did. Very well set up and capped off by our tour guide and his personality. Long drive and a good stop over at the 'egg shell' picture factory

The extensive network of tunnels, trapdoors and hiding places illustrates the incredible adaptability of the Viet Cong guerrillas, as well as the harshness of the war.The exhibits, photos and informative tours provide an insight into the construction and strategic use of the tunnel system. It made us think to see the ingenuity and deprivation of everyday life in the tunnels. The Cu Chi Tunnels are a memorial to the horrors of war and an important testimony to Vietnamese history.

isiting the Cu Chi Tunnels provides a sobering, eye-opening look at the realities of the Vietnam War. The vast network of underground tunnels demonstrates the extraordinary ingenuity and resilience of the Viet Cong guerrilla forces. Crawling through a small section of tunnel highlighted the challenges and sacrifices made by those who lived underground.The exhibits, short films, and knowledgeable guides offer a detailed historical overview of the tunnels' role and construction. While not a light-hearted experience, Cu Chi sheds light on a crucial aspect of the conflict. We found it a powerful and thought-provoking reminder of the human cost of war.

We visited this site as it was included in an agency tour.I must admit that I was very disappointed with the visit, let me explain, this is a place that has entered the legend of the history of Vietnam, a place where there was fierce fighting and where many people suffered and died. From my point of view it should be a place to be visited with respect, almost a shrine to honor his memory. Instead it has been transformed into a sort of amusement park where tourists of all kinds and nationalities seem to want to vent their war instincts in a sort of children's war game, it has even been set up a shooting range to complete the picture where you shoot at targets in uniform with all due respect to peace.

For those with good knowledge of the Vietnam war, this is just a one sided Viet Cong propaganda day camp for kids. The presentation is simplistic and outdated. All of it is fake and could have been setup 15 minutes from Saigon instead of requiring a 4 hour road trip in the middle of nowhere and wasting the whole day.

An excellent perspective of how clever the locals of Cu Chi were in hiding from the enemy and protecting their land. While most are just recreations, being able to experience  the tunnels was confronting, even for just 40 metres. Having the shooting range nearby was disturbing but gave the place  is a bit of reality of  what happened  during the war. Worth the  trouble of the visit.
The Cu Chi Tunnels were excellent. Amazing to see.  We did a private tour there through Blueriver Tours via boat and it was fabulous and our tour guide was very informative . Highly recommend!

We squeezed through those narrow passages, and suddenly our apartment felt HUGE.  Seriously, though, the ingenuity is incredible – kitchens, hospitals, living quarters… all underground!Warning: it's dark, tight, and a bit scary picturing what life must have been like down there.  However, we gained a whole new appreciation for the resilience of the Vietnamese people. Bring a flashlight and a sense of humor, and you'll be amazed at the history hiding beneath the surface.

Astonishing! Incredible underground organization in the middle of nowhere. You understand better why it has been impossible for the G.I.s to clean up that area. Very impressive the exposition of traps that were hidden in the bushes.

A fantastic visit here today. Superb tour guide with us made it so enjoyable. Maybe bring a bottle of water with you as you do some walking around and it gets very warm. 34c today.  The surface underfoot is uneven in places so be careful. Toilets are very clean.  .

Situated supposedly on top of a real Viet Cong tunnel complex but not impressive - a few replica buildings, some tunnel entrances showing how tight a fit they were, which could have been real or replica, some airholes, which could have been real or replica, some mantraps, which could have been real or replica, and a short stretch of tunnel which was supposedly real but enlarged for tourists. The Viet Cong tunnel system was / is one of the wonders of the modern world but this site does not do it justice.

Its a tourist destination to learn about being physically and mentally strong. To be obsessed for freedom. its not easy to fight for 20 long years staying in so small tunnels in middle of War with Americans... Respect to the soldiers !

I have visited the Cu Chi Tunnels twice - there are 2 locations and my word of advice is to choose the larger site. I did this on my first visit and recently went to the smaller site which was not nearly as good. Admittedly it was the weekend so likely to be busy, but busy is an understatement for such a small site (compared to the other). We were moved on by the tour guide as there was no time to wait for others to finish viewing/listening at certain locations which made it feel rushed and difficult to get a true sense of place (given the incredible history). The larger site was quieter and left more time for exploring and going down into tunnels. At both sites you are able to try machine guns (definitely not something I would do - it just did not feel appropriate). the issue I had with the smaller site was the noise - at the midway point of the tour they leave you at a cafe for 10mins but the gun range is right next to it and the noise is unbearable. They even leave ear defenders out! The sites offer an insight into the incredible stories and plight of those that fought in the war and I feel that the larger site did that justice. A chance to engage and truly imagine what life may have been like. The smaller site felt very much a tourist destination rather than having significant historical value. If I were rating the smaller site I would only give 3/5.

Paid extra for the quieter tour and I thought it was worth it. There were less groups of people. Decided on the morning tour as I thought the day would be less hot. The areas you walk through outside were pretty shaded so it wasn’t actually too bad. Cuong or “Joe” was one of the best tour guides I’ve had on a tour. He was very funny, intelligent, knowledgeable and passionate. I am grateful that he shared so many personal stories with us. This was an incredible learning experience thanks to him.

Must watch place in when you visit HochiMinh city. Very special historical background of the war between Vietnam and USA.Tunnels are very special attractions and walking inside is very good experience.Shooting the M16 and other guns makes this tour very special.

These well-preserved tunnels are a testament to Vietnam's ingenuity during the Resistance war. With escape routes cleverly disguised and camouflaged, we couldn't help but marvel at the craftiness of it all.As we squeezed through the narrow passageways, we imagined being sneaky soldiers, albeit with less at stake and more giggles. It's not every day you get to play historical hide-and-seek in a war relic! The tunnels, once a symbol of resilience, now a playground for our couple's caper, had us chuckling at the thought of popping up like groundhogs in a game of whack-a-mole.Our visit wasn't just about the past; it was about creating our own funny memories in a place that's seen so much.Here's to history with a side of humor!

This is a very interesting site of how the tunnels were dug and used. I felt sorry for the American soldiers as it would have been so hard to fight in these circumstances. With the booby traps and tight tunnels.  For the people having to live in them life would have been so tough but they were so resourceful.
This really is something everyone should see. I am not wild about enclosed spaces so did not go into the tunnels but still a lot to see and do without feeling you are missing out.
The tunnels are simply amazing. We learned and experienced a lot through the tour. I really enjoyed the trip to the Cu Chi Tunnels!

Be open minded. There’s two sides to any argument. War is unpleasant. The tunnel network is one aspect, the gory traps are unpleasant. Shooting live ammunition might be your thing. But imagine trying to live underground and take guerilla warfare to the enemy.
Fantastic day together with Marcus. So many interesting facts about ho chi minh history and Vietnam War and a great lunch mid day. Thank you!

Visited the chu chi tunnels on the 24/2/24 and booked through Vietnam adventure tours. We were picked up on a very comfortable air conditioned bus. Our tour guide Tuan was extremely knowledge of the area and the war as he personally fought in the war. Tuan was extremely friendly,funny and helpful. 10/10 Would recommend going and choosing Tuan as a guide.

Part of a river cruise. Excellent tour where park rangers let you crawl thru the tunnels used by the North Vietnam soldiers during the war. Fascinating

Traveling through these narrow tunnels was a unique experience, allowing us to better understand the living conditions of Vietnamese soldiers during the War of Resistance. This is a long trip from Saigon.  There is a lot of anti American propaganda to endure to visit a collection of holes, booby traps and a short crawl through a tunnel. Amazing service and good tourguide. Safe driver and allnall a great day.Alot of information and well organized tour through the tunnels. So glad I took the time and did this! Nice with only a few persons to, so we had better time and opurtunity to se and ask questions.
The trip was great. My tour guide, Mr. Tuan was excellent, he knows about this very well and very detail gave the information about the war.

Step into history with a journey through the Cu Chi Tunnels, an extraordinary marvel showcasing guerrilla warfare tactics of the past. This unique attraction offers a truly immersive experience as we delved deep underground, navigating the intricate web of tunnels. Marvel at the ingenuity of the Vietnamese soldiers who designed and built a network spanning over 250 kilometers. With a mix of historical information and interactive displays, the Cu Chi Tunnels provide a fascinating insight into the challenges faced during the war. A must-visit destination for history enthusiasts and adventurous travelers alike.

Tangled in ruins and overcoming numerous difficulties and dangers - Vietnam's widespread resistance network of the Communist Party of Vietnam marks our monumental journey. Once a secret base during the Resistance War, located under the lush green Cu Chi area, the 250-kilometer-long Cu Chi tunnel system has become a famous relic. Under the guidance of a tour guide, we entered this maze-like tunnel system and were amazed by the perseverance of the Vietnamese people. The hospital hidden underground only adds to our awe of this rich history. What we are proud of is that we successfully avoided the mine traps in the tunnel. It was really a rare adventure experience.

Visit through this original aerial theater of firefights. There is still a tank with bullet holes. You see the terrible traps used by the Vietnamese and you descend into the small, dark, narrow tunnels. Now only those on the first level remained but our guide strictly wearing the uniform of that period told us that we went down three levels. Some tunnels stretched from Saigon to Cambodia.

You can gain extensive insights into the Vietnam War and explore the tight tunnels. It's a great destination for a half-day trip from Ho Chi Minh City, and you can easily incorporate it into your overall city tour.

Cu Chi Tunnels is an underground defense system in Cu Chi district, 70km northwest of Ho Chi Minh City. This system was dug by the Viet Minh resistance army and the National Front for the Liberation of South Vietnam during the Indochina War and Vietnam War. The tunnel system includes an infirmary, many rooms, a kitchen, a warehouse, a working room, an underground tunnel system, about 250km long and has ventilation systems at the location of the bushes. Cu Chi Tunnles were built at the end of the Ho Chi Minh Trail, on land known as “stell land” to praise the resilienet defense will of the people and soldiers here. During the 1968 Tet Offensive, the Southern Liberation Army departed from this tunnel system to attack Saigon. After the war, the Cu Chi tunnels became a national historical site. In 2015, the Cu Chi Tunnels historical relic site received the title of Hero of Labor for its particularly outstanding achievements in labor and creativity. On February 12, 2016, the relic received the Special National Monument ranking certificate

It’s a mustAmazingUnbelievable to see. Great experience. Sad at times and disturbing but a must. Shooting was brilliant but expensive 10 bullets £18 aproxTunnels are smallI am big and did 18metersLoved it.

We went there with our tour guide who had immense knowledge about the Vietnam War. It’s worth seeing the place. My husband & our other tour mates entered the tunnel it was worth the experience. They have a shooting range too I had my lifetime experience of shooting an AK47 🥰.Never seen a place like this tunnel in my lifetime, so  I will always remember it ❤️They served us food too in the restaurant was good we enjoyed 🥰So I will recommend a must-visit to Cu Chi Tunnel for all tourists visiting Hochiminh vietnam👍

there are 2 war tunnels currently open for public visit- Ben Dinh : 1.5h north from hcmc. more touristy, shooting range onsite- Ben Duoc: 2h north from hcmc, or another 30 min further on from Ben Dinh. less touristy, buggy ride to shooting range, visit Ben Duoc temple and also observe other aircrafts used in war onsite things to know- the war tunnels at both sites have already been widened to accommodate most regular tourist size. only 20-30% visitors choose to enter the tunnel. claustrophobia is the main reason deterring one. the tunnel i went in at Ben Duoc was much longer (>50 m) and also descends to 2nd floor. it is possible that Ben Dinh has the same, but just that i wasnt led to one by a guide. - the main advantage of Ben Duoc is it has less tourists. relatively speaking anyway, since most organized tours go to Ben Dinhprice- entry tickets for each war tunnel is 125,000 VND- I arranged a private car hire with a Grab driver i met the day before. expect to pay around 1,200,000 - 1,500,000 VND (or 50-60 USD at the time of writing) for a full day ride from hcmc (e.g., 7 am - 2:30 pm).  if you plan to visit both sites, make sure you stop at Ben Dinh first. that place is like a zoo after 10 am. that's when all the organized tours arrive. - at the shooting range, all the rifles are already bolted down for safety reasons. it costs 600,000 VND for 10 bullets, and 10 bullets is the minimum purchase. it is quite expensive, so try to share it with another tourist. apparently they only allow a max of 2 tourists to share 10 bullets (or so they claim). i was lucky enough to shoot a couple of rounds on AK47 complimentary of another tourist. summary- having been to both sites, i can attest that it is not necessary to visit both. this is because the 15 min introductory film, demo tunnel for tourists to crawl though, mannequin exhibits, guided tour content, etc, are all similar if not identical at both sites. so it is somewhat repetitive. but the war tunnels are worth a visit
This tour turned out more fun than I thought and gave some unique experiences exploring life and tunnel system of Vietcong. The shooting range was a nice entertainment!

Ling was an extremely amiable and knowledgeable tour guide who went above and above to ensure that our trip was both instructive and pleasant. I found the experience and knowledge to be quite valuable in comprehending the struggles and accomplishments of the Vietnamese people.

We went to this place during our tour of Ho Chi Minh City. These tunnels were used for military operations during the war  against USA by vietnamese soldiers . We did a last minute visit  to the Cu Chi Tunnels on Wednesday 6th December. We got a grab from our hotel in District 1 who took us straight there for about £20 (each way) and then it was really reasonably priced to enter the tunnels (125,000 each so just over £4 each). My daughter, who is 8 years old, absolutely loved it and there were a few younger children there. We didn’t have a tour guide with us but managed to get around the site ok. The tunnels of Củ Chi  are an immense network of connecting tonnels located in the 60 km close Saigon), Vietnam, and are part of a much larger network of tunnels that underlie much of the country. The Củ Chi tunnels were the location of several military campaigns during the Vietnam War, and were the Viet Cong's base of operations for the Tet Offensive  in 1968.Location of Củ Chi tunnels in Ho Chi Minh CityThe tunnels were used by Viet Cong soldiers as hiding spotsduring combat, as well as serving as communication and supply routes, hospitals, food and weapon caches and living quarters for numerous North Vietnamese fighters. The tunnel systems were of great importance to the Viet Cong in their resistance to American forces, and helped to counter the growing American military presence.

With a length of 250 kilometers, the tunnels are equipped with living quarters, kitchens, meeting rooms and even hospitals. Worth a visit not amazing

The Cu Chi Tunnels are a fascinating site demonstrating the strength, courage, and resilience of those that once occupied this land. It evidently holds so much history and has been witness to so many stories that everyone visiting should be able to feel like they're experiencing and reliving. Regrettably, I think the site falls short of providing this experience for some of those visiting.

We visited as part of a group tour, and found on arrival that the majority of tours from Saigon arrive at around the same time. Being on a weekday, this also included large school groups, so particularly the early stages of the tunnels were absolutely packed with people. We had to move through these parts quickly and come back to them later to avoid the crowds, and we missed a lot of what our guide was saying as a result. This experience is also negatively impacted by the shooting range that has been introduced. While I'm sure there are some tourists that would enjoy this addition, for most it is just awful. Shots can be heard across the site during the entire visit, and at the midway refreshment stop, can be deafening. We saw multiple groups with fingers on ears such was the noise. It's a really horrible addition. The second half of the tour was better as the larger groups dispersed and filed out, and there was more space available to explore and experience the site properly. For those managing the tunnels or tour operators, thinking about staggering arrivals would help to limit the impact that the crowds have on the experience. I also think more can be done to really allow those visiting to feel and imagine what being here during the war would have been like.

For those visiting, I would recommend either arriving first thing in the morning, or leaving it until the afternoon to visit. We left around midday and it was significantly quieter than when we arrived around 10am. There is an audio guide available, which I would imagine provides a lot of valuable information that can be used as an alternative to a planned tour. Overall, I think this is a fantastic site that should offer a lot more than it does.

Eigentlich ein Muss für jemanden, der tiefer Eindrücke des Vietnamkrieges einsteigen wahrnehmen möchte. Es ist leider recht touristisch. Genau da muss man abwägen, ob Infogewinnung wichtig ist oder eher ruhig und beschauliche Location..

Ciekawe miejsce chociaż liczyłem na więcej atrakcji. Tak uczciwie to lepiej zjeść coś ciekawego w Ho Chi Minh niż tutaj przyjechać. Pieniądze wyrzucone w wietnamskie błoto.

Had a really bad experience with a guide who pressured us to exit the tunnels early and then left us alone for the last leg, telling us that we were on our own.

We actually started our tour really nicely. We booked through SST Travel and Kyle, the guide who was assigned to us, was great. He gave good explanations, friendly and gave adequate bathroom breaks. (The tunnels are about 1.5-2hours from District 1, so the latter is pretty important.)

At the tunnels, on-site guides in green uniforms are there to guide you through the tunnels. Kyle told us to closely follow the guide in green while inside the tunnels. From previous explanations, we know that there are 3 main exits: 20m, 40m and 100m. At the first stop, our guide already said we should exit. We're a group of 8 people and no one had any trouble, in fact, we were excited to move on. The guide continued but moved so fast that he was soon out of sight. We came to an intersection and didn't know where to go. Ahead looked to be a dead end so we retreated to try to go left, which ended up being an exit. Exasperated, we called out to the guide. He replied "I'm here" without any further explanation. We tried the 'dead end' which ended up going upwards (hey, at least we got to experience being fooled by the tricky tunnels; they are truly amazing). At the second main exit, our guide said it was time to leave. We said we were fine to go through to the end. No one was panicky and our group was made up of young-ish members and pre-teens. There was no reason to pressure us to stop.

The guide then said he was leaving and we could go on to the end by ourselves. When I said I didn't know the way, he said there was only one way and no other exits. When I tried to ask more, he said I was holding up the line and said if anything happened to us from that point, it was our own fault. I seriously contemplated leaving but the kids were excited so I led the group to the finish line, which was honestly not far away.

I want to emphasis that no one in our group was having trouble and since the guide was out of sight most of the time, he wouldn't have known either way. The most stressful thing about being in the tunnels was not the confined space or thick air, it was how the guide treated us. I'm also wondering if it's against regulations to leave visitors in the tunnels by ourselves. After exiting, my son said, "Let's not let a bad guide spoil our experience". I'm trying to take his advice because the place is truly awesome.

Username: Royalbliss Holidays And Hospitality Pvt Ltd
Comment: One time see

Cu Chi Tunnels offer a fascinating glimpse into Vietnam’s wartime history and impressive underground ingenuity. However, it’s located quite far from Ho Chi Minh City expect over two hours each way in heavy traffic, which makes the trip feel long and tiring. The shooting range was also a bit of a letdown, not as exciting or well-organized as expected. Most bullets for automatic guns were soldout. Overall, it’s worth visiting for the history, but be prepared for a long commute and some underwhelming parts of the experience.

Najfajniejszą atrakcją tego miały być tunele. Jak się okazało tunel został powiększony wybetonowany i zrobiony prosto pod turystów. Moja sugestia jak ktoś się wybiera najlepiej wziąć coś na kolana. Bo sposobem który pokazywał przewodnik jeśli jesteś dość wysoki daleko nie zajdziesz i najwygodniej śmigać na kolanach. A przez to że wszystko jest wybetonowane kolana bolą. Wyjścia są co 20 metrów także jak ktoś by nie dawał rady można wcześniej skończyć. W ostatniej części tunelu jest węziej niż w całej reszcie ale nie ma tragedii. Do tego cały tunel jest oświetlony. Ogólnie ciekawe doświadczenie. Chociaż pozostaje niedosyt że nie są to orginalne tunele. W które pewnie miałbym problem się zmieścić. Do AK47 jest minimum 10 ja strzelałem z RPD to minimum w taśmie było 20 nabojów to już był koszt 1200000 dongów. Także dość drogie to strzelanie. Oprócz tych dwóch broni było jeszcze M16, M60 i K93 niestety do M60 skończyły się im naboje. Fajnie wyglądały też pułapki. Ogólnie była to wycieczka z biura turystyczne i dojazd w jedną stronę zajął 3 godziny w sumie dojazd w obie strony zajął więcej niż czas spędzony tam.  Oprócz tego był jeszcze postój gdzie wyrabiali obrazy ręcznie bardzo piękne ale mega drogie. W hcmc na targu takie same można kupić za 1/10 ceny.

The Cu Chi Tunnels, the biggest attraction of Ho Chi Minh City I was told. These are the tunnels where the Viet Cong fought off the Americans during the Vietnam War. I joined a tour so I can get the history that goes with these tunnels. There's some very interesting history here indeed. The tunnels are not in their original form. They have been widened for tourists. There's a gun range where you can fire automatic weapons but you'll have to pay for the bullets. That's not my cup of tea. I paid a bit more to have a smaller group tour (12 participants) thinking that it would make the tour a bit more enjoyable and the atmosphere a bit more intimate. Ultimately, that made little difference because it was so overcrowded at the site, everyone was competing with everyone else to experience the tunnels and hear the history of the war. I did not overly enjoy this tour. It's inundated with tourists and it felt like we were too much of a hurry. This was no fault of the tour company or the tour guide. In fact, our tour guide was pleasant, engaging and very fluent in English. Now I wish whichever organization owns this tourist attraction would maybe put a cap on the number of tourists onsite at any given time so it maybe more enjoyable. I had asked the other participants in my group what they thought of the tour. One declined to answer as our guide was closeby at the time. That spoke louder than words. This obviously was my experience. Not everyone had the same experience I did and that's Ok. Besides that, I thought the tunnels were interesting. I tried going through them and got claustrophobic in mere minutes and had to get out. That was an experience.

Ich wollte ursprünglich mit dem Bus fahren, aber in Anbetracht des Verkehrs in HCMC habe ich ein Grab Fahrer (Motorroller - 1.000.000) gebucht, der gleichzeitig mein Guide war. Die Fahrt dauerte 1,5h - rückzu etwas schneller. Der Eintritt kostet 125.000 Dong. Die Anlage war überfüllt - es war schwer Fotos zu machen. Die ganze Zeit habe ich nur "Geballer" gehört. Die Anlage wirkte etwas unauthentisch.
Ich hätte mir lieber die Ben Duoc in der Nähe angeschaut, als die Cu Chi Tunnel. Jedoch war mein Fahrer Feuer und Flamme, dass ich schießen soll. Ehrlich - ich besuche doch keinen Kriegsschauplatz und schieße dort. Es gibt auch Essen - übrigens neben dem Schießstand.

Interessante geschiedenis, maar wel heel erg toeristisch. Bij elk punt moet je even wachten en de schietbaan is een toeristische attractie. Mocht je langer de tijd hebben, dan is de tour bij de Vinh Moc tunnels (tussen Phong Nha en Hue) een absolute aanrader!
Un peu déçu de la visite après avoir fait 1h30 de route depuis Ho Chi Minh City. Je ne trouve pas que ça vaille le coup si vous n’êtes que de passage dans cette ville.

Wenn man einen kurzen Aufenthalt in HCMC hat empfehle ich die Reise zu den Tunneln nicht anzutreten, da die Hin- und Rückfahrt 5 Stunden plus 20min Pause beträgt, sowie viel Verkehr in der Stadt. Im Januar war es sehr voll, viele Reisegruppen stehen an den einzelnen Stationen der Führung. Des Weiteren ist es konstant ziemlich laut, da man die Schüsse des Schiessstandes hört.
Außerdem haben wir ca. 45min gewartet bis Leute unserer Reisegruppe fertig waren mit schiessen. In der Nähe des Schiessstandes, wo wir gewartet haben, ist es natürlich umso lauter. Das Erlebnis der Tunnel und Fallen, sowie das Leben im Krieg war interessant aber man kann das sicher auch sehr gut im Kriegsreste Museum in der Innenstadt nachempfinden und man spart sich viel Zeit und Geld.
No recomiendo ir en Enero. Muchísimas personas

Tourist trap tbh - coach loads of visitors all day. Interesting site historically, but the horrendously long journey out of HCMC makes the day less enjoyable. Some tour guides also make you stop at a state run factory by disabled persons where they try to get you to buy their craft.

Es bonito y con mucha historia. Te explican a breves rasgos lo que fue la guerra del Vietnam; tampoco es tan extenso ni tan divertido.
Nosotros vimos un tanque y algunas trampas.
Si quieres disparar debes pagar como 20 dólares y solo es un tiro.
En general lo recomiendo si quieres ir a pasar el rato.
Great place to visit. Must do. HOWEVER, pls do not buy any BULLET souvenirs. They are NOT ALLOWED on flights. We had to throw all the beautiful looking souvenirs

Absolut sehenswert. Aber nicht mit get your guide oder einer anderen gebuchten Tour.
Fahrer nehmen, selbst hinfahren, durchlaufen.
Alles andere dauert ewig und es lohnt sich nicht.

Don't believe the gift shop keepers on the site when they say you can take the souvenirs made out of bullets with you, home. Total lie. It's still considered as an ammunitions and they will take it at the airport. Learnt it the hard way!

Un buen lugar, con mucha historia, pero con poco orden, caos de gente y un guía con poca onda.

Contratamos la excursión ofrecida por Kim travel , después de dos horas de viaje por el tráfico, llegamos al lugar. Nos encontramos con un caos de gente tanto en el ingreso como en el recorrido de la excursión, esto no nos permitió parar en todos los puntos de interés, porque estaba repleto de guías con grandes grupos por todos lados. Nosotros no tuvimos la suerte de tener un guía con ganas de trabajar y predispuesto a encontrar la forma de que veamos todo, así que nos salteamos bastantes puntos de interés hasta llegar al sector donde podes disparar armas que parece ser el atractivo del lugar. Podes disparar armas de la guerra vietnamita a un valor de 2.50 usd por bala. Luego de estar un rato en el lugar, nos dirigimos a los famosos túneles, en donde tuvimos la oportunidad de ingresar en ellos recorriendo casi 200m.

LO RECOMIENDO, pero yendo por tu cuenta. No saques ninguna excursión, podes tomar un taxi o un gran desde la ciudad, llegarás más rápido y podrás ver todos los puntos de interés. La entrada tiene un valor de 120.000

If you want to go there better take a tour together with Mekong River trip. In another way it isn’t worth it: the way there from Saigon is too long and the time spending there is too short. I mean there is nothing much to see accept the tunnels. You can also practice in shooting but I’m not a fan that’s why I had to wait for others from my group for like one hour listening to the crazy, loud, and disturbing sounds of gun shots.
Anh dẫn viên làm rất tốt 👍

A historic place from vietnam's point of view. Extremely touristy with food offerings being quite OK. You can try the firing range but it can get quite expensive. All in all a checklist item on your visit to HCMC.

The guide from Kim’s guides was  the boss and couldn’t recommend him enough, Kelvin was the man!!
Look I’m no angel but shoot me with an AK for falling asleep during the documentary at the end and turning my nose up when they tried to feed us the meals the viet Cong had to eat. They say it’s all part of the experience but I say it’s 2024 could I grab a Pho with a cold beer plz boss?
Guess which one I am ?

מתחם משוחזר של מנהרות ומלכודות הויטקונג במלחמת ויטנאם ארצות הברית,הנסיעה מסייגון אורכת כשעתיים לכל כיוון הביקור במקום לא יותר משעה וחצי.

Es una visita que te recomiendan mucho pero no cuanta demasiado, está demasiado explotado turisticamente y no consigue explicar bien la historia de este lugar, pierde  un poco su esencia por ser tan artificial.

1: Tra trên GG thì vé 35k/ng, nhưng lúc mua vé thì 100k, sau đó mới biết là người bán vé tự ý bán thêm vé tham quan khu vực mới (chỗ này có giá 65k). Đúng ra nên hỏi trc khách tham quan có muốn tham quan cả 2 không.

2: Đi trong địa đạo thì rất ít nhân viên hướng dẫn, toàn phải đi kiếm mấy ổng để hỏi đường đi. Tới chỗ nắp “ẩn thân chi thuật”, vợ mình hỏi có xuống được không, cha nội hdv muốn ưu tiên đoàn nước ngoài ổng đang dẫn (dù tụi mình tới trước) nên dụ tụi mình “chút nữa cũng có chỗ giống vậy, anh chị lên kia đi”. Sau đó mới biết ko có chỗ nào khác nữa. Thứ lừa đảo!

3: Tới chỗ nhà bếp đc ăn khoai mì miễn phí (đã bao gồm trong vé), nhưng ai không hỏi thì mấy chị im luôn (cũng ko để bảng cho khách biết). Nhờ hồi nhỏ mình đi rồi mình biết nên hỏi, mấy bả đi vòng ra sau bếp lấy cho tụi mình. Khoai dở ẹc ah, nhưng giờ dẫn con vô đây thuyết minh cho nó thì cho nó cắn miếng cho biết hồi xưa dân mình khổ thế nào. Chắc mấy chị nhân viên cũng muốn tụi mình thấm sự khổ sở này :)

Tunnel Cu Chi
Leider nur die Touristen Anlage. Es gibt zwei Tunnelanlagen die man besichtigen kann. Wir waren in der Ben Dinh Anlage. Die Anlage wurde für Touristen nachgebaut. Man erhält zwar auch einen Eindruck über das harte Leben der Partisanen und Bauern und ihren Kampf, aber ist halt zum Durchschleusen von Touri-Massen. Mit Souvenir Shops und zum Schluss einer Shooting Ranch, auf der man mit scharfen Waffen schießen kann! Da wird geballert, daß die Rohre glühen. Den Lärm hört man schon weit bevor man da ist. Halten wir für eine solche Anlage, die ja eigentlich zum Gedenken da ist, nicht für angemessen.
Wenn man die Wahl hat, dann lieber die Anlage 20 Km weiter Nördlich – Ben Duoc- besuchen. Da sind die Originalanlagen, die überwiegend von den einheimischen besucht werden. Keine Touri Massen! Ohne Shooting Ranch und derartigen Kommerz. Und wer dann noch mehr über den Krieg erfahren will sollte ins War Reminant Museum in Saigon gehen. Eindrucksvoll!

Incredibile che nel tour ci sia anche un poligono di tiro: acquisti le munizioni e ti eserciti a sparare. Pazzesco! Piuttosto dovrebbe esserci un luogo dimostrativo in cui si possano distruggere le armi e i simboli della guerra.

I feel really torn with this review. The tunnels themselves and all the associated history is for sure 5 star - and one of the absolute best things you can do while exploring Vietnam. The reason for the lower rating is that our local guide was very disappointing. He kept hurrying us along, would start speaking before everyone had arrived and clearly did not really care about making sure everyone in the group had a great experience. Perhaps our experience was this way because we took a Grab up and did this whole thing in a DIY way. Maybe we should have booked a tour from the city where you not only get dedicated transport up and back but also a dedicated tour guide that's likely to care more than the local onsite ones. Also food and water are difficult to come by up there so bring your own water and snacks if you can.

Interesting to see but quite a hike (4.5 hours return motorbike trip) for what was about 45 minutes of actual experience (we didnt do paintball or shooting range). Local guide was friendly but rushed us through the tunnels. They make you watch a 10 min video from 60s before you start. They didnt bother to show us the kitchen bunker. In total we did 4x tunnels of different length, 5m, a 10m, a 50m, and a 15m. They offer a local food snack at the end. Tunnels are narrow but you can get through them, only the long one is a bit more challenging as ceiling is lower and has a few bends. You never go below 3-4m below ground. These arent the only tunnels in the area, we realised there were others such as Ben Dinh (we didnt visit).

Hôm nay mình có dịp đến thăm khu di tích lịch sử Địa đạo Củ Chi bằng xe bus, mình khởi hành tại trung tâm Văn hoá quận 12 và bắt xe 74 đến bến xe Củ Chi, tiếp đó leo lên xe 79 chạy thẳng 1 đường tới cổng khu di tích.
Cả 2 tuyến xe đều khá ít người đi nên chất lượng hơi xuống cấp và cũ kĩ.
Ở Địa đạo có các khu trưng bày hiện vật, chiến tích chiến tranh, kèm theo là bài thuyết giảng sơ của HDV về sơ đồ, thời gian hình thành và quy mô của địa đạo Củ Chi. Sau phần thuyết minh thì chúng ta sẽ được hướng dẫn đi vào các hầm ( có các loại như hầm 5m, hầm cứu thương,hầm 50m... Và được giới thiệu về ống thông hơi, giếng nước sinh hoạt, hầm chông,... ). Về phần quà lưu niệm thì giá thành ngang bằng các khu di tích khác, tuy nhiên khá ít mẫu hàng, hàng cũ tồn kho còn nhiều nên nhìn không được bắt mắt. Có khu vực căn tin bán snack ( bịch bánh tráng mè khá ỉu nhưng cũng to 40🐟/bịch), nước giải khát ( 20 🐟/ chai ). Chỗ bán đồ ăn trưa thì giá thành khá cao ( 55 🐟/ 1 dĩa cơm );( 50🐟/ tô hũ tiếu ) ăn tạm được nhưng không ngon. Khu vực trưng bày món ăn làm mình khá mất thiện cảm vì để đồ ăn trưng bày lâu dẫn đến tình trạng mốc meo, làm mình mất hứng ăn. Tuy nhiên thức ăn được chuẩn bị dưới bếp sẽ luôn là đồ tươi ngon ( này là mình nghe bạn mình nói vậy ), nên cũng không cần quá lo lắng đâu.
*Góp ý: là bên chỗ bán đồ ăn nên chụp hình sản phẩm rồi in trực tiếp vào menu cho nó trực quan và tiết kiệm 1 lượng chi phí, chứ để mốc meo mà trưng bày ra mình ớn ớn quá.

So good historical place to experience ! their tunnels are amazing and gives a great experience .The Cu Chi Tunnels are an extensive underground network used by the Viet Cong during the Vietnam War, stretching about 250 kilometers (155 miles). They were hand-dug with simple tools and served multiple purposes including hiding spots, communication routes, living quarters, weapons factories, field hospitals, and bomb shelters. The tunnels had a sophisticated system of tunnels on three levels, with the deepest level over 12 meters underground, designed to withstand heavy bombings, chemical warfare, and enemy attacks.
Interesting facts about the Cu Chi Tunnels include that they connected villages and communes, effectively creating an underground city with kitchens, hospitals, schools, theaters, and music halls. The tunnels featured clever camouflage with hidden trapdoors, ventilation shafts, and bamboo traps to injure enemy soldiers known as “Tunnel Rats.” Narrow entrances were designed specifically for Vietnamese soldiers, making it difficult for enemies to enter. During the war, the tunnels allowed the Viet Cong to launch surprise attacks and survive relentless bombing campaigns.
These tunnels were initially dug in the 1940s to resist French colonial forces and later expanded during the Vietnam War against American and South Vietnamese troops. Today, some sections of the tunnels are open to the public for exploration, showcasing a remarkable testament to the resilience and ingenuity of the Vietnamese fighting forces as they used the tunnels not just for combat but also to maintain daily life underground amid wartime hardships.

ベトナム・ホーチミン市郊外に位置するクチトンネル（Địa đạo Củ Chi）は、単なる地下壕ではなく、ベトナム戦争（第二次インドシナ戦争）におけるベトコン（南ベトナム解放民族戦線）の不屈の抵抗精神を象徴する、歴史的に極めて重要な史跡です。この場所を訪れることは、戦争の技術的な側面と、人間の生存戦略の極限を体感する、他に類を見ない旅です。

この巨大なトンネル網は、1948年の対フランス戦争期から始まり、20年近い歳月をかけて拡張されました。ベトナム戦争において、ここは解放戦線の作戦本部として機能し、その全長は一説に250kmにも及ぶ迷路のような地下要塞都市へと進化しました。
実際にトンネルの一部に潜入すると、当時の生活と戦闘のリアリティが迫ってきます。居住スペース、食料貯蔵室、野戦病院、さらには台所までが地下深くに設けられていたという事実は、ベトコンが「地下で生活し、地下で戦う」という極限の生存戦略を確立していたことを証明しています。この構造の緻密さと忍耐力こそが、ベトナム戦争の行方を左右した、重要な歴史的要因でした。

地上に展示されている、解放戦線の兵士たちが使用した様々なブービートラップは、当時の戦いの生々しさと残酷さを伝えています。その原始的でありながら巧妙な仕掛けからは、技術的に優位な敵（米軍）に対抗するための知恵と決意が強く感じられ、当時の戦争の苛烈さが目に浮かぶようです。
そして、この史跡の特異な点は、射撃場が設けられていることです。ライフルやマシンガンといった実銃を撃つ体験は、なかなか他ではできない、戦争という非日常を垣間見る貴重な機会を提供します。これは、歴史を学ぶだけでなく、当時の戦闘の音と衝撃を追体験するという、倫理的な問いをも含む、深遠な経験となるでしょう。

クチトンネルは、ベトナムの歴史の不屈の精神が刻まれた、「生きた戦争遺産」です。壮大な自然の造形を見る旅とは異なり、ここでは人間の極限的な知恵と意志を体感できます。ベトナム戦争の歴史を深く学び、その影響を肌で感じたい訪問者にとって、必見の、そして挑戦的な場所と言えます。

For those wanting to learn about the history of Vietnam (and in particular the Vietnam war) then the Cu Chi Tunnels are well worth the visit. The only negative for me was a rude tour guide who stood in front of me as I was about to take a photograph and asked for $5 (USD) stating “my country, my rules”. It can also be quite busy, but I didn’t find this an issue.

A very interesting experience. It takes about 1.5 hours from Ho Chi Minh City to reach the Cu Chi Tunnels, which feels like quite a drive considering you spend around 2–3 hours at the site before heading back. Still, it is probably worth it to see the tunnel network, the different traps, and to gain more background on the history.

I highly recommend going with a guide. We had Beo, who was excellent, not only explaining the history of the war and the tunnels but also providing insight into Vietnam today and its future. Very engaging and informative.

It is also fascinating to crawl through one of the tunnels yourself. They are narrow, but manageable as long as you are not claustrophobic.

There is also a shooting range nearby, which adds to the atmosphere since you can hear gunshots in the background. I strongly recommend bringing earplugs, because it gets very loud as you get closer. Even if you go to the range itself, earplugs on top of the provided headphones make it much more comfortable. One thing to keep in mind is that you need to buy at least 10 bullets, which cost 60,000 VND each, so about 20 euros in total. Unfortunately, you cannot share them with others, which is a pity because I would have liked to split them with my travel companion. When we were there, only a handgun and a carbine were available, not all the weapons listed at the counter.

Overall, a fun and interactive experience to better understand how things were during that time. Definitely recommended!

Had a wonderful afternoon with Ms. Sunny from Vietnam Adventure Tour, booked via the Viator app. Choosing a non-touristy small group was a great decision — minimal queue for the Cu Chi Tunnels and even for the ladies' washroom!

Ms. Sunny was engaging, always smiling, and spoke excellent English. She, along with the very capable driver, made the experience smooth and enjoyable. Despite being briefly stopped by the police (for the usual “coffee money”), the driver kept everything efficient and got us back to Ho Chi Minh City on time for dinner — which I really appreciated!

The afternoon tour was an excellent choice — the weather was cooler and more comfortable. I even had the unique experience of firing an AK-47 — half a magazine cost 1.3 million VND, and it was totally worth it for the adrenaline rush.

A well-organized and memorable tour. Highly recommended!

最近透過KKday預訂了胡志明市古地道的團體行程，總體感覺非常不錯！這是我第一次參加這種主題旅行，體驗非常特別。古地道位於叢林內，環境清幽，溫度比想像中涼爽許多，即使第二天去也沒有感到太熱，穿著輕便衣服走一圈都很舒服。導覽員的解說也很詳細，介紹了越南戰爭時期的歷史和地道的使用情況，讓人對這段歷史有了更深的了解。沿途還有機會參觀地道內的陷阱和生活設施，感覺很有沉浸感，特別推薦給喜歡歷史或冒險旅行的朋友。
不過，體驗射擊的環節有點讓人失望。雖然能試用真槍是個亮點，但槍支都是固定在牆上，無法感受到後座力，體驗感打折扣。更重要的是價格真的有點貴，差不多30美元才10發子彈，對於想多玩幾次的遊客來說負擔不小。如果能調整價格或提供更多彈藥選項，應該會更吸引人。團體行程的安排整體還算順暢，交通和時間控制都OK，KKday的服務也方便快捷，下次還會考慮用這個平台預訂其他活動。
總體來說，胡志明市古地道值得一遊，尤其是透過KKday組團很省心，環境和歷史價值加分，只是射擊環節可以再優化。建議帶好水和防曬用品，享受這趟有趣的旅程！

Questa cittadella sotterranea ha raggiunto finalmente la pace, diventando una attrazione turistica. È un esperienza che ti fa capire come i viet gong, siano riusciti a vincere  la guerra più  con l'ingegno che con i mezzi. In modo didattico sono riprodotti le trappole, i trabocchetti, i sistemi con cui riciclaggio tutto ciò  che trovavano. Esperienza consigliata, ma non andateci il sabato e i festivi,  potreste trovare un numero impressionante di turisti.

Je potrebné vidieť aj tieto tunely, kde sme pochopili čo sú  ľudia schopní podstúpiť a vymyslieť, aby prežili. A ich presvedčenie a odhodlanie muselo byť nezlomné. No červený sever a "demokratickejší " juh sa nemajú stále moc v láske. No človek získa predstavu o tom, čo sa tu odohrávalo. O strašných veciach, ktoré sa tu stali sa mi nechce písať. Boli zverstvá na jednej, aj druhej strane, no Vietnamci boli doma a verili, že si vybojujú lepšiu budúcnosť. Nie je to ani tu celkom tak.

The Cu Chi Tunnels are definitely worth adding to your itinerary. It’s not just an adventurous stop, it’s also an important historical site that offers a powerful glimpse into the Vietnam War. Walking through the area really puts into perspective the resilience and resourcefulness of the Vietnamese people who lived and fought within this underground network.

The visit is both informative and engaging. You’ll see preserved tunnels, examples of booby traps, artillery remains, and different types of weapons used during the war.

One of the highlights is the chance to actually go inside sections of the tunnels. It’s a unique experience and gives you a small sense of what life might have been like underground. That said, the tunnels are quite narrow in places, so anyone who is claustrophobic should keep that in mind.

Even if you are claustrophobic, I would still strongly encourage you to try the first tunnel. It is only about 10 metres long and is relatively spacious compared to the much narrower sections found in the later tunnels.

The tunnels are located roughly 1.5 hours from Ho Chi Minh City, so joining a half-day guided tour is usually the easiest option. A guide can explain the historical background, point out important details around the site, and make the visit much more meaningful than going on your own.

Located North West around 50 kilometres from Ho Chi Minh airport this is one of the unique, unforgettable and profound places we visited in Vietnam. It takes roughly 1.5 to 2 hours from the airport mainly because of high traffic snarls in the city.

Its thought provoking and seriously makes one think about the necessity of peace and calmness required in world today for human race to survive.

The booby traps and hidden underground bunkers, reportedly spread in a network, across a total length of 250 kilometres approx, was made self sufficient for the survival of the Vietnamese guerilla soldiers, who were in a do or die situation with American soldiers in the war. The tunnels barely walkable (said to be made wider for the tourists to walk or squeeze in), are severely claustrophobic and had rooms, clinics, kitchens, dining hall, meeting rooms, shooting spots, ammunition storages and what not. The tunnels connected to the river directly to enable the soldiers escape surface and travel directly by water ways.

A highly recommended place for visit in the entire Vietnam.

Found myself a spot on a joining tour to Ben Dinh Cu Chi tunnels. Our tour guide Khang is very knowledgeable and cared for all fifteen of us getting our clips/shots as well as our safety. There were many other visitors but it wasn't super crowded. Really interesting to learn about all the traps used as well as the ways air, food, and water were sustained in the tunnels! There were two tunnels - the first one I had to bend my torso down for the bulk of it. The pictures online were kinda deceiving, I thought I could just walk normally through the tunnels! There is a guide who leads the group too. Mind you that I'm a 1.68m Asian - I saw a bunch of taller visitors leaving within the first 10m of the first set of tunnels as they felt claustrophobic or hot. There is one part towards the end of the first set of tunnels where you have to crawl to get to the end.

The second set of tunnels was NOT illuminated, and I had to crawl all the way. I guess this one is closer to the experience of the viet cong soldiers during the war. It was dark, cooling, tight. There were bends and some drops/steps to work through. I don't think I can get another experience like this elsewhere!

You can choose to crawl through both tunnels, the first one only, or none. There is also a small cafe near the range, and 2 souvenir shops. Towards the end we were served STEAMED CASSAVA/TAPIOCA WHICH IS SO GOOD I LOVE IT! I highly recommend visiting to immerse yourself in the wartime experience!

Very Visiting the Cu Chi Tunnels was one of the most powerful and eye-opening experiences of my Vietnam trip. This incredible underground network offers a deep insight into the resilience, intelligence, and courage of the Vietnamese people during the Vietnam War. Walking through the site, you truly appreciate how soldiers lived, worked, and survived for years in extremely challenging conditions.

The guided tour is informative and engaging, explaining the ingenious tunnel design, hidden trapdoors, ventilation systems, and daily life underground. Crawling through a section of the tunnel—though optional—gives a real sense of how narrow and intense life must have been. The preserved bunkers, command rooms, and war relics make history feel very real and personal.

The site is well maintained, organized, and surrounded by greenery, which contrasts sharply with the stories of hardship shared during the tour. Facilities are decent, and guides are knowledgeable and respectful. Overall, the Cu Chi Tunnels are not just a tourist attraction but a moving historical lesson. A must-visit for anyone interested in history, culture, and understanding Vietnam beyond its beautiful landscapes.

Cu Chi Tunnels is an extremely interesting and meaningful place to visit. There are two main sites, Bến Đình and Bến Dược, managed by Cu Chi and the central government (Sai Gon) respectively. While both sites share the same core tour content, such as the historical documentary film, explanations of tunnel structures, different room types, and the reasons the tunnels were built, the overall experience at each site has some differences.

At both locations, the tour provides very detailed and vivid information about life underground. You can see how narrow and complex the tunnels are and even crawl through a short section yourself, which really helps you understand how challenging life was. The displays of traps, weapons, and manufacturing processes feel very real, giving a strong sense of how people lived, fought, and survived. Visitors can also try traditional wartime food such as boiled manioc with salt and sesame, and experience firing replica guns similar to those used during that period.

At Bến Đình, as a Vietnamese visitor or if you understand Vietnamese at a high level, you only need to buy a normal entrance ticket. There are free guided tours in Vietnamese that run periodically. You just need to ask the staff at the entrance. There is no need to purchase an additional tour guide ticket. This surprised and impressed me quite a lot, as the explanations were detailed and well organized.

At Bến Dược, guided tours are provided by default, so the experience feels more structured from the beginning. In addition to the main tunnel tour, Bến Dược offers deeper insight into civilian life before and during the war. One of the most impressive highlights is the large sand table display with lights, smoke effects, and storytelling, showing how the liberation zones were formed, how intense the bombings were, and what people had to endure. I also watched a 3D film that powerfully illustrated the impact of bombs on the land and on human lives.

Bến Dược is also home to the Bến Dược Temple, a memorial dedicated to honoring the soldiers and civilians who sacrificed their lives in the resistance wars. Visiting the temple offers a quiet and reflective space to pay respect, read the names and stories of those who were lost, and better understand the human cost behind the history presented in the tunnels. It adds an important emotional and cultural dimension to the visit and helps complete the overall experience.

Beyond the historical exhibitions, Bến Dược also has more interactive and recreational activities such as paintball shooting, swimming, and cultural performances, making it suitable for both education and hands on experiences.

Overall, everything felt incredibly real and impactful right in front of my eyes. Củ Chi Tunnels is not just a historical site. It is a powerful reminder of resilience, creativity, and survival. I truly loved this place and highly recommend it to anyone who wants to understand an important part of Vietnam’s history.

​While some of the weapons on display can be a bit confronting, they are an important part of the history. The tunnels themselves are extremely narrow and can be a bit scary for children, so we didn't go all the way through, but it was still a great experience.

Vietnam is a country that truly captures the heart of anyone who visits. The people of Vietnam are among the most welcoming, humble, and kind-hearted individuals I have ever met. Their warmth and hospitality make visitors feel at home from the very first moment. Whether in the bustling streets of Hanoi or the peaceful villages along the Mekong Delta, there’s always a smile to greet you and a helping hand ready when needed.

The culture of Vietnam is equally rich and inspiring—a perfect blend of tradition, resilience, and grace. Deeply rooted in family values, respect for elders, and community spirit, Vietnamese culture reflects a harmony between the past and the present. Festivals, music, and art beautifully showcase the nation’s history and pride, while its cuisine—fresh, flavorful, and balanced—perfectly mirrors the Vietnamese way of life: simple yet deeply satisfying.

What stands out most is the people’s positive spirit and their ability to find joy and strength even in challenges. Their respect for nature, tradition, and human connection is truly admirable.

Overall, Vietnam’s people and culture leave a lasting impression—a reminder that kindness, simplicity, and unity are the true essence of a beautiful life.
